Option 0 vagueness increases inconsistent handling of ambiguous cases. Option 2 omits the guidance the agent needs. Option 3 wastes context and distracts from the task.
referenceText: Be clear and direct: explicitly state the role, goal, available tools, and constraints to get reliable, consistent behavior.

stem: The agent should be able to consult the current returns policy document, which is read-only reference material. In MCP terms, how should this be modeled versus the actions the agent performs?
options: Expose the returns policy as a tool with side effects, like process_refund
Expose read-only reference material such as the returns policy as an MCP resource, while state-changing actions like process_refund remain tools
Treat resources and tools as identical, since MCP makes no distinction between them
Conclude that policy documents cannot be provided to the model at all
rationale: Correct: MCP resources are for read-only context/data, while tools are for actions with effects; the policy doc fits resources and refunds fit tools. Option 0 wrongly makes reference data an action tool. Option 2 is false; MCP explicitly distinguishes resources from tools. Option 3 is false; documents can be provided as resources.
referenceText: MCP resources expose read-only data/context to the model, while tools represent actions the model can invoke.

stem: Leadership wants to know whether the agent truly meets the 80%+ first-contact resolution target with correct escalation. What is the best way to measure this?
options: Measure only average response latency and ignore whether the issue was actually resolved
Build an eval set of labeled scenarios and measure first-contact resolution rate plus escalation correctness (precision and recall) against ground truth
Count the number of tool calls per conversation as the sole quality metric
Have the model grade its own transcripts as the only measure of success
rationale: Correct: a labeled eval set with resolution rate and escalation precision/recall directly measures the stated goals. Option 0 latency ignores correctness. Option 2 tool-call count is a weak proxy unrelated to resolution quality. Option 3 unchecked self-grading is unreliable as the sole signal.
referenceText: Define success with representative, labeled test cases and measure task-specific metrics rather than proxies.

stem: A team lead notices that every developer has to re-explain the same build commands, code style, and testing conventions to Claude Code at the start of each session. She wants Claude to load these conventions automatically for everyone who checks out the repo. What is the most appropriate mechanism?
options: Commit a CLAUDE.md file at the repository root containing the conventions, build commands, and style guidance
Ask each developer to paste the conventions into the first prompt of every session
Store the conventions in a .env file so Claude reads them as environment variables
Create a custom slash command that developers must run before doing any work
rationale: Correct: a project-level CLAUDE.md checked into the repo root is automatically loaded into context at session start and shared across everyone who clones the repo, making it the canonical place for build commands, style, and conventions. Option 1 is exactly the repetitive burden being eliminated. Option 2 .env is for environment variables, not model memory. Option 3 still requires a manual, remembered action every session.
referenceText: Claude Code memory / CLAUDE.md is loaded automatically at session start and shared via the repository.

stem: An architect is designing the CLAUDE.md memory strategy for a large monorepo. Which statements about how Claude Code loads and composes memory files are correct? (Select 2.)
options: A CLAUDE.md in a subdirectory can supplement the project-root CLAUDE.md when Claude works with files in that subtree
A CLAUDE.md file can pull in additional files using @path imports so shared guidance is not duplicated
Only one CLAUDE.md file may exist per repository; any additional files are ignored
Memory files are re-parsed from disk on every single tool call to guarantee freshness
CLAUDE.md is read only when the user explicitly runs a /memory command
rationale: Correct: Claude Code supports a memory hierarchy including project-root and nested subdirectory CLAUDE.md files that apply when working in that subtree (0), and files can use @path imports to include other files (1). Option 2 is false; multiple memory files across the hierarchy are supported. Option 3 is false; memory loads into context at session start, not per tool call. Option 4 is false; project/user CLAUDE.md loads automatically.
referenceText: Claude Code memory supports a hierarchy of files and @path imports; files load automatically at session start.

stem: A developer is about to have Claude Code perform a risky, multi-file refactor of authentication logic in a production repository. Before any files change, she wants Claude to research the codebase and present a step-by-step approach that she can review and approve. Which Claude Code capability directly supports this?
options: Headless mode with the -p flag, which runs the refactor unattended and non-interactively
Plan mode, which investigates and proposes an approach without editing files until the plan is approved
The /clear command, which resets conversation context before Claude begins editing
An auto-accept setting that applies each edit as soon as Claude proposes it
rationale: Plan mode is built for read-and-propose workflows: Claude investigates and produces a plan without applying edits until the user approves, which fits a high-risk refactor that needs review first. Headless -p is for unattended execution, the opposite of review-before-change. /clear only wipes conversation context. Auto-accept applies edits immediately, removing the review gate entirely.
referenceText: Claude Code common workflows (plan mode)

stem: A team wants a reusable '/security-review' command that runs the same detailed prompt against whatever file path the developer passes in. Where should they define this command, and how should the file reference the passed-in path?
options: Add a shell alias named security-review in the developer's profile that pipes the file to Claude
Define it as an npm script under scripts.security-review in package.json
Create .claude/commands/security-review.md containing the prompt and reference the path with $ARGUMENTS
Place the prompt under a '## security-review' heading in CLAUDE.md so it loads every session
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files in .claude/commands/; the filename becomes the command name and $ARGUMENTS injects whatever follows the command, so /security-review src/auth.ts passes that path in. A shell alias cannot drive a Claude session prompt. An npm script is unrelated to slash commands. Text in CLAUDE.md is always-on context, not an invokable parameterized command.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands and $ARGUMENTS

stem: A DevOps engineer wants Claude Code to run in a CI pipeline: given a diff, it should generate a summary and exit non-interactively, printing only its final text output. Which invocation is appropriate?
options: Script keystrokes into the interactive TUI to type the prompt and capture the screen output
Run claude -p "summarize this diff" so it executes non-interactively and prints the final result
Start an interactive session and use /compact to shorten the summary before copying it out
Use plan mode so the pipeline can approve the plan before the summary is emitted
rationale: The -p (print) flag runs Claude Code in headless mode, executing a single prompt and emitting output suitable for scripting and CI. Scripting keystrokes into the TUI is brittle and unsupported for automation. /compact summarizes conversation context, not a CI mechanism. Plan mode requires interactive human approval, defeating unattended CI.
referenceText: Claude Code CLI reference (headless / print mode)

stem: A team wants Claude to automatically respond to '@claude' mentions in pull request comments, make the requested changes, and push commits back to the PR branch. What is the intended way to set this up?
options: Give each reviewer a personal API key and have them run Claude locally on every pull request
Add a CLAUDE.md instruction telling Claude to watch the repository for @claude mentions
Enable plan mode so the PR author can approve Claude's edits from the terminal
Install the Claude Code GitHub Action and configure it with an API key stored as a repository secret
rationale: The Claude Code GitHub Action is purpose-built to react to @claude mentions in issues and PRs, run in GitHub's runners, and commit changes, configured via repository secrets. Manual local runs provide no mention-triggered workflow. A CLAUDE.md instruction cannot make Claude poll GitHub. Plan mode is an interactive terminal feature unrelated to PR comments.
referenceText: Claude Code GitHub Actions

stem: A developer wants Claude Code to query the team's Sentry issues and Postgres schema during debugging sessions, using existing servers that expose those capabilities over the Model Context Protocol. What is the correct approach?
options: Paste the Sentry and Postgres documentation into CLAUDE.md so Claude can infer the current data
Rebuild each server's functionality as custom slash commands under .claude/commands
Register the MCP servers with Claude Code (e.g., claude mcp add) so their tools and resources are available in sessions
Have Claude open a browser and sign in to Sentry manually at the start of each session
rationale: MCP lets Claude Code connect to external tools and data through standardized servers; adding them (claude mcp add or config) exposes their tools and resources to the model. Pasting docs gives static text, not live query access. Slash commands are prompt templates, not a live tool interface. Manual browser login is not how Claude integrates data sources and is unreliable.
referenceText: Claude Code MCP integration

stem: An engineering team wants to build a custom autonomous coding agent — running in their own service, not the Claude Code CLI — that reuses Claude Code's agent loop, tool execution, and permission handling. Which offering is designed for this?
options: Shell out to the Claude Code binary from their service for every incoming request
Build on the Claude Agent SDK, which exposes the same agent harness — loop, tool execution, and permissions — for custom agents
Follow the prompt engineering guide's few-shot examples to emulate the agent loop
Encode the desired agent behavior in a large CLAUDE.md that their service loads
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK provides the same underlying agent harness (loop, tool use, permissions, context management) that powers Claude Code, so teams can build custom agents in their own applications. Shelling out to the CLI is fragile and not the supported programmatic surface. Few-shot examples are a prompting technique, not an agent framework. CLAUDE.md only configures an existing Claude Code session.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK overview

stem: A developer needs Claude to extract structured metadata (title, owners, severity) from free-text incident reports and return it so downstream code can parse it reliably without regex-scraping prose. What is the most reliable technique?
options: Ask Claude in prose to 'return JSON' and parse whatever it emits
Set temperature to 0 and rely on the model to always emit valid JSON on its own
Include one example incident in CLAUDE.md and ask Claude to imitate its formatting
Define a tool with a JSON schema for the fields and have Claude call it so output conforms to the schema
rationale: Defining a tool with a JSON schema and having the model call it constrains output to the declared structure, the recommended path for reliable structured output. Asking for JSON in prose yields inconsistent, sometimes malformed output. Temperature 0 increases determinism but enforces no schema. A CLAUDE.md example is weak conditioning that does not guarantee structure across varied inputs.
referenceText: Tool use for structured output

stem: Claude Code keeps generating commit messages in an inconsistent format even though CLAUDE.md says 'write good commit messages.' Applying prompt-engineering best practice, what is the highest-leverage change?
options: Repeat 'write good commit messages' several times in CLAUDE.md for emphasis
Replace the vague instruction with explicit formatting rules plus two or three example commit messages
Switch to a smaller, faster model to reduce output variance
Raise temperature so the model explores a wider range of message styles
rationale: Vague directives like 'write good' underspecify the target; being explicit about the format and providing a few concrete examples (few-shot) is the core technique for consistent output. Repetition adds no specification. A smaller model does not fix an underspecified prompt. Higher temperature increases variance, worsening consistency.
referenceText: Prompt engineering: be clear and direct

stem: An architect is defining a tool for Claude to call during automated refactors. Which practices improve the reliability of the tool-use interaction? (Select 2.)
options: Omit the description to keep the token count low and let the model infer usage
Write a detailed tool description covering what it does, when to use it, and the meaning of each parameter
Give every parameter the type 'string' and rely on the tool name to convey intent
Provide a JSON schema with typed, well-named parameters and mark the required fields
Have the model emit the call as a Markdown code block instead of using the tool-use mechanism
rationale: Detailed tool descriptions and precise, typed JSON-schema inputs with required fields are the most important factors for reliable tool use — the model chooses and fills tools based on these. Omitting the description starves the model of guidance. Loosely-typed strings remove the structure that prevents malformed calls. Emitting a Markdown code block bypasses the structured tool-use API and forfeits schema validation.
referenceText: Tool use best practices

stem: During a long generation task, Claude Code produces a plausible-looking config value but the developer suspects it was invented. Which prompting practice most directly reduces this kind of fabrication?
options: Instruct Claude to always sound confident so the output reads cleanly
Ask the same question three times and take the majority answer
Allow Claude to say it is unsure and give it tools to look the value up in the codebase rather than guess
Increase max output tokens so Claude has room to elaborate on the value
rationale: Giving the model explicit permission to say 'I don't know' plus tools to verify (e.g., reading the file) is a standard technique to reduce hallucination in agentic settings. Encouraging a confident tone increases the risk of confident fabrication. Majority voting does not help when the model consistently invents the same value. More output tokens do nothing to ground the value.
referenceText: Prompt engineering: reduce hallucinations

stem: Halfway through a large feature, a Claude Code session's context is cluttered with a completed, unrelated debugging tangent, and responses are drifting. The developer wants to keep working on the feature but shed the irrelevant history. What is the best action?
options: Continue the session and rely on the model to disregard the stale tangent
Run /clear to reset the conversation, then reintroduce only the feature's relevant context
Delete the project CLAUDE.md to free up room in the context window
Lower the model's temperature to offset the accumulated noise
rationale: /clear resets the conversation so accumulated, irrelevant context stops degrading responses; the developer then reintroduces only what the current task needs. Continuing lets stale context keep causing drift. Deleting CLAUDE.md removes valuable persistent guidance and doesn't address the polluted conversation. Temperature does not remove irrelevant context.
referenceText: Claude Code managing context (/clear, /compact)

stem: A team runs Claude Code headlessly to triage failing tests overnight. They want it to attempt an automated fix, but if it cannot resolve an issue after a bounded effort, it must stop and record what it tried instead of thrashing indefinitely. Which design best supports this?
options: Instruct Claude never to stop until every test passes
Remove all permissions so Claude cannot modify any files during the run
Define explicit stop conditions and an escalation path — after N attempts, write findings to a report and exit
Run at maximum temperature so it tries more varied fixes on each iteration
rationale: Reliable agentic workflows need bounded effort, clear stop conditions, and an escalation path that captures structured state when the agent cannot succeed. 'Never give up' invites infinite loops and wasted spend. Removing all permissions prevents the intended automated fix. Max temperature adds randomness, not a stopping rule.
referenceText: Building reliable agents / stop conditions

stem: An architect is designing a multi-hour Claude Code migration that will exceed a single context window. She wants the agent to survive context compaction and resume without losing track of which files are done. What is the most robust pattern?
options: Rely on the model retaining the full conversation history for the entire run
Have the agent maintain an external structured progress file (a checklist/log) it reads and updates as it works
Disable /compact so the context window never shrinks during the migration
Keep progress only in terminal scrollback for the developer to consult
rationale: Persisting progress in an external structured state file makes long tasks resilient to context loss and compaction, letting the agent resume accurately. Relying on conversation memory fails once context is compacted or exceeded. Disabling compaction is not viable for tasks larger than the window. Terminal scrollback is not machine-readable state the agent can act on.
referenceText: Context management with structured state

stem: A developer reports a bug where an API returns 500 intermittently. She wants Claude Code to investigate methodically. Which prompting approach best fits Claude Code's debugging workflow?
options: Tell Claude only 'the API is broken, fix it' and let it start editing immediately
Ask Claude to rewrite the entire service from scratch to sidestep the bug
Share the error message and stack trace and ask Claude to reproduce, locate the root cause, and propose a fix before editing
Paste the full repository into one prompt and ask for the answer
rationale: Effective debugging starts by giving Claude the concrete signal (error text, stack trace) and asking it to reproduce, isolate the root cause, and propose a fix — a targeted, evidence-driven loop. A vague 'it's broken' prompt yields unfocused edits. A full rewrite is disproportionate and risky. Dumping the whole repo wastes context and buries the signal; Claude can navigate the repo itself.
referenceText: Claude Code common workflows (debugging)

stem: A security-conscious team runs Claude Code in CI and wants to restrict which tools it may use without a human approving each action, allowing only reading files and running the test command. Which mechanism is appropriate?
options: Rely on plan mode to block unwanted edits during the automated run
Add a prompt instruction asking Claude not to delete or modify anything unexpected
Set a high temperature so Claude behaves more cautiously
Configure allowed tools via permission settings (e.g., --allowedTools) so only sanctioned actions run without prompting
rationale: Claude Code's permission system (settings and the allowed-tools configuration) is the supported way to constrain which tools run without prompting, essential for safe non-interactive CI. Plan mode is interactive, not a CI permission control. A polite prompt instruction is not an enforceable guardrail. Temperature has no relationship to permissions or safety.
referenceText: Claude Code settings and permissions

stem: A staff engineer is deciding what belongs in the project CLAUDE.md versus a one-off prompt. Which items are appropriate, durable content for CLAUDE.md? (Select 2.)
options: The commands to build, test, and lint the project
The one-time task 'rename getUser to fetchUser in auth.ts today'
Coding conventions and architectural patterns the team expects Claude to follow
The full contents of every source file so Claude never needs to read them
Debug output captured from this afternoon's failing test run
rationale: CLAUDE.md is for durable, project-wide guidance that should always be in context: build/test/lint commands and coding conventions/architecture. A single one-time task belongs in a prompt, not persistent memory. Pasting every file's contents bloats context and duplicates what Claude can read on demand. Transient debug output is ephemeral clutter.
referenceText: Claude Code memory best practices

stem: An architect wants a slash command that generates release notes and must always return them in a fixed sectioned template (Features, Fixes, Breaking Changes). To make the structure reliable across runs, what should the command's prompt include?
options: A single instruction: 'make nice release notes'
A note telling Claude to be creative and vary the structure each run
The exact template with its headings plus one or two filled-in example release notes
A reminder to keep the notes concise without specifying the sections
rationale: Providing the exact template and a couple of worked examples (few-shot) is the most effective way to lock in a consistent, sectioned output format. A vague one-liner underspecifies the format and yields drift. Encouraging variation undermines the fixed-template requirement. A brevity reminder without naming the sections does not constrain the structure.
referenceText: Prompt engineering: use examples (multishot)

stem: When Claude Code calls a tool that fails (for example, a test command exits with an error), what is the recommended way to give the model a chance to recover within the agent loop?
options: Swallow the error and return an empty result so the model continues optimistically
Return the tool's error output to the model as the tool result so it can read the failure and adjust its next step
Terminate the session on any tool error without passing information back
Convert the error into a success result so the agent loop keeps moving
rationale: Feeding the actual error output back as the tool result lets the model observe what went wrong and correct course — the core of resilient tool-use loops. Swallowing the error hides the signal and leads the model to build on a false premise. Hard-terminating forfeits recovery for fixable failures. Faking success corrupts the agent's world model and guarantees downstream mistakes.
referenceText: Tool use: handling tool result errors

stem: A team lead sets shared coding standards in the repository's ./CLAUDE.md, while an individual engineer keeps personal shortcuts in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md. On the engineer's machine the two files give conflicting guidance about test-runner commands. When the engineer runs Claude Code inside the repo, how does Claude Code resolve which memory applies?
options: Only the project ./CLAUDE.md loads; the user-level file is ignored whenever a repository CLAUDE.md exists
Only ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md loads, because personal memory always overrides project memory
Both files load and combine; where they conflict, project memory takes precedence over the user's personal memory
Neither loads automatically; the engineer must import each with an explicit @path reference at session start
rationale: Claude Code loads all applicable memory files (enterprise, project, then user) and combines them, with higher-level (enterprise/project) memory taking precedence over user memory when guidance conflicts, so the shared project standard wins. Both files are read, not one. User memory does not override project memory — precedence runs the other way. Memory loads automatically by discovery, so no manual @path import is required.
referenceText: Claude Code memory precedence: enterprise and project memory take precedence over user memory

stem: A platform team maintains a canonical set of API conventions in docs/api-guidelines.md and wants every service repo's CLAUDE.md to pull in that guidance without copying the text into each file. What is the intended Claude Code mechanism?
options: Rename docs/api-guidelines.md to CLAUDE.local.md so Claude Code auto-discovers it as extra memory
Paste the full guidelines into a fenced code block in CLAUDE.md so they are treated as authoritative
Register the guidelines file as an MCP resource that Claude fetches each turn
Add an import line such as @docs/api-guidelines.md inside CLAUDE.md so its contents load with memory
rationale: CLAUDE.md supports importing other files with @path syntax, letting shared content live in one place and be pulled into memory. Renaming to CLAUDE.local.md does not import another file and that pattern is deprecated in favor of imports. Pasting the full text defeats the single-source-of-truth goal, and imports inside code spans are explicitly not evaluated. An MCP resource is a different mechanism for tool/data access, not for composing memory files.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md files import additional files with @path/to/import syntax; imports are not evaluated inside code spans

stem: An engineer opens Claude Code in a large existing repository that has no CLAUDE.md yet and wants Claude to generate an initial project-memory file summarizing structure, build commands, and conventions. Which action is designed for this?
options: Run /compact to distill the current repository into a memory file.
Paste the full directory tree into the prompt and ask Claude to persist it as memory.
Run /init to scan the codebase and scaffold a CLAUDE.md file.
Start Claude Code in headless mode with --output-format json so it writes memory during the run.
rationale: /init is the built-in command that scans the project and scaffolds a CLAUDE.md with structure, commands, and conventions. /compact summarizes the current conversation context, not the repo, and creates no memory file. Manually pasting the tree is unnecessary and error-prone. --output-format json only controls how headless output is serialized and does not generate memory.
referenceText: Use /init to bootstrap a CLAUDE.md for your codebase.

stem: Mid-session, an engineer discovers a non-obvious rule ("always run migrations before seeding") that Claude keeps forgetting across turns. They want to persist it to memory quickly without leaving the session or manually opening a file. What is the fastest supported way?
options: Wrap the rule in triple backticks so Claude Code appends it to CLAUDE.md automatically.
Begin a line of input with # and type the rule; Claude Code prompts for which memory file to store it in.
Prefix the rule with ! so Claude Code runs it and records the result to memory.
Run /clear and then type the rule so it is preserved in the compacted summary.
rationale: Starting an input with # is the built-in shortcut to add a quick memory; Claude Code asks which memory file to store it in. Triple backticks denote a code span and are not treated as memory. The ! prefix runs shell commands, not memory saves. /clear wipes conversation context and would discard the rule rather than persist it.
referenceText: Quickly add a memory by starting your input with the # character; you'll be prompted to select which memory file to store it in.

stem: A team wants a reusable /fix-issue command that takes an issue number and injects a standard triage prompt. They create .claude/commands/fix-issue.md. Which two statements about authoring this custom slash command are correct? (Select 2.)
options: The file must be valid JSON with a top-level "prompt" key or Claude Code will not register the command.
Reference the text after the command name with the $ARGUMENTS placeholder to substitute it into the prompt.
The command's file name must match a built-in command in order to override it.
Saving the file under .claude/commands/ makes it a project command available to anyone who clones the repo.
Custom commands may only invoke read-only tools and cannot declare a description or argument hint.
rationale: Custom slash commands are Markdown files whose body becomes the prompt, and $ARGUMENTS captures everything typed after the command name. Files under the project's .claude/commands/ directory are project-scoped and shared via source control. Commands are Markdown, not JSON. They support optional frontmatter (description, argument-hint, allowed-tools) and can be granted tools, so the read-only/no-metadata claim is false. Names need not match built-ins.
referenceText: Custom slash commands are Markdown files in .claude/commands (project) or ~/.claude/commands (personal); $ARGUMENTS captures passed arguments; frontmatter supports description, argument-hint, and allowed-tools.

stem: An engineer writes a custom command that should first run git status and embed its output in the prompt, then ask Claude to summarize uncommitted work. They want the git command to actually execute when the command is invoked. What must the command file include?
options: An @ prefix referencing git so Claude Code shells out automatically at load time.
A tool_choice: bash frontmatter field, which auto-approves any shell command in the body.
Nothing special; any backticked shell snippet in the body runs before the prompt is sent.
A line beginning with ! to run the command, plus allowed-tools frontmatter granting the Bash tool.
rationale: In a slash command, a line prefixed with ! executes a bash command and embeds its output, but this requires allowed-tools frontmatter that includes the Bash tool (optionally constrained to specific commands). The @ prefix references file contents, not shell execution. There is no tool_choice: bash frontmatter field. Backticked snippets are inert text, not executed.
referenceText: Commands can execute bash before the prompt runs using the ! prefix, and must include the Bash tool via allowed-tools frontmatter; @ is used to reference files.

stem: One engineer keeps a personal /scratch command that only makes sense for their own workflow and should never appear for teammates or be committed. Where should the command file live?
options: In the repo's .claude/commands/scratch.md with frontmatter visibility: personal so teammates never see it.
As scratch.local.md in .claude/commands/, which Claude Code keeps out of git but shares with the team.
At ~/.claude/commands/scratch.md, registering it as a personal command across all your projects.
As scratch.CLAUDE.md at the repo root so it loads as per-user memory rather than a shared command.
rationale: Personal commands live in ~/.claude/commands/ and are available to that user across every project without being part of any repo. Putting the file in the repo's .claude/commands/ makes it a shared project command, and there is no visibility: personal frontmatter that changes that. The *.local.md and *.CLAUDE.md naming schemes for commands are fabricated. Scope is determined by directory, not a filename.
referenceText: Commands in ~/.claude/commands are personal (available in all your projects); commands in a project's .claude/commands are project commands shared with the team.

stem: An engineer must refactor authentication logic spread across an unfamiliar service and is worried Claude will start editing files before the approach is understood. They want Claude to investigate and propose a step-by-step plan first, making no changes until approved. Which Claude Code capability fits?
options: Auto-accept edits mode, so each edit is applied immediately as Claude works through the code.
Plan mode, which limits Claude to read-only analysis and presents a plan for approval before any edits.
Headless mode with --output-format json, so the proposed plan is emitted as machine-readable output.
The /compact command, which trims context so Claude can reason over the whole refactor at once.
rationale: Plan mode constrains Claude to read-only exploration and produces a plan you approve before execution, matching the safe-investigation workflow. Auto-accept edits mode does the opposite. Headless JSON output governs serialization for scripting, not a plan-first guarantee. /compact only summarizes conversation context and does not gate edits.
referenceText: Plan mode lets Claude research and produce a plan without modifying files; you review and approve before it executes.

stem: A reviewer claims plan mode is unsafe because Claude might still write to disk while exploring. Which statement most accurately characterizes plan mode's guarantee?
options: Plan mode permits edits but automatically reverts them via git if the plan is rejected.
Plan mode disables network access only, while file writes proceed as normal.
In plan mode Claude Code is limited to read-only operations and will not edit files or run mutating commands.
Plan mode behaves like normal mode except that Claude's reasoning is hidden until the plan is complete.
rationale: Plan mode is intentionally read-only: Claude gathers information and drafts a plan without mutating the workspace, so the concern is unfounded. It does not permit edits-then-revert; it prevents the edits in the first place. It is not merely a network toggle, and it is not normal mode with hidden reasoning.
referenceText: Plan mode restricts Claude to read-only actions so it can analyze and plan without changing files.

stem: During an interactive session an engineer wants to switch from the default behavior into plan mode, and later into auto-accept-edits, without retyping their request. What is the intended way to move between these interaction modes?
options: Press Shift+Tab to cycle through the available modes, including plan mode and auto-accept edits, during the session.
Restart Claude Code with a different --mode flag, since interaction modes cannot change mid-session.
Set a mode: field in CLAUDE.md, which takes effect on the following turn.
Run /model and pick a mode, since interaction modes are tied to the selected model.
rationale: Shift+Tab cycles through interaction modes such as plan mode and auto-accept edits without leaving the session. Modes are switchable live, so a restart with a flag is unnecessary. There is no mode: field in CLAUDE.md governing this, and /model selects the model, not the interaction mode.
referenceText: Use Shift+Tab to cycle interaction modes, including plan mode and auto-accept edits.

stem: A CI job needs Claude Code to generate a changelog from a diff and exit, with no interactive prompt and no waiting for human input. Which invocation is designed for this non-interactive, scriptable use?
options: Run claude and pipe "yes" into stdin so the interactive REPL auto-confirms each step.
Run claude -p "<prompt>" to execute a single request in headless print mode and exit.
Run claude --interactive with a detach flag so the REPL runs in the background.
Run claude /init in the pipeline, since project bootstrapping is the supported CI entry point.
rationale: The -p (print) flag runs Claude Code headlessly for a single prompt and exits, the supported pattern for CI and scripting. Piping "yes" into the interactive REPL is brittle and not intended. There is no --interactive detach mode. /init bootstraps memory and is unrelated to running arbitrary CI tasks.
referenceText: Use claude -p (print mode) to run Claude Code headlessly for non-interactive and CI use.

stem: A pipeline runs Claude Code headlessly and a downstream script must reliably parse the result, including intermediate events for a live log. Which two flag choices support this? (Select 2.)
options: Pass --output-format json to receive a single structured result object for parsing.
Pass --output-format markdown, which guarantees a machine-parseable schema for each turn.
Pass --output-format stream-json to receive newline-delimited JSON events as they occur.
Rely on the default text output and parse the human-readable transcript with regular expressions.
Pass --raw so the script receives the model's raw tokens without any envelope.
rationale: --output-format json emits a structured result object for reliable parsing, and --output-format stream-json emits incremental JSON events, which a live log needs. There is no markdown output format guaranteeing a schema. Regexing default text is the fragile approach structured output replaces. --raw is not a Claude Code output format.
referenceText: --output-format supports text, json, and stream-json; json returns a structured result and stream-json emits events incrementally.

stem: A team wants everyone who clones their repo to automatically get the same MCP server (an internal issue tracker) configured in Claude Code, checked into source control. Which configuration achieves shared, project-scoped MCP setup?
options: Have each developer add the server with a --local flag so it is stored per-machine outside the repo.
Put the server definition in ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md so it loads as user memory on every machine.
Define the server inside a slash command file so it registers whenever the command runs.
Commit a .mcp.json file at the project root defining the server, which loads as project-scoped MCP config.
rationale: A checked-in .mcp.json at the project root is the project-scoped mechanism that shares MCP servers with everyone who clones the repo. A local scope is deliberately per-user. CLAUDE.md holds memory/instructions, not MCP server config. Slash command files are prompts, not MCP registration.
referenceText: Project-scoped MCP servers are configured in a .mcp.json file checked into the repository and shared with the team.

stem: An architect is explaining why Claude Code uses the Model Context Protocol rather than hardcoding each integration. Which statement best captures MCP's role?
options: MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how applications expose tools, resources, and prompts to LLMs, so one client can connect to many servers through one interface.
MCP is a proprietary Anthropic API callable only by Claude models, replacing standard tool use.
MCP is a file format for persisting conversation memory between Claude Code sessions.
MCP is a linting protocol that validates generated code before it is written to disk.
rationale: MCP is an open standard for exposing tools, resources, and prompts to LLM applications, letting a client like Claude Code integrate many servers uniformly. It is not proprietary or Claude-only, not a memory file format, and not a linting protocol.
referenceText: The Model Context Protocol is an open protocol that standardizes how applications provide context (tools, resources, prompts) to LLMs.

stem: A developer is building a helper that must return classification results as strict JSON (label plus confidence) that a program consumes directly. They want to make it hard for the model to reply with prose. Using the Claude API tool-use pattern, what is the most reliable approach?
options: Ask for JSON only in the prompt and raise temperature so responses vary less.
Instruct the model to wrap the JSON in a Markdown code fence and post-process it with a regex.
Define a tool with an input_schema for the JSON shape and force it with tool_choice so the model returns matching arguments.
Send the request without a system prompt so the model defaults to structured output.
rationale: Defining a tool with an input_schema and forcing it with tool_choice makes the model emit arguments conforming to your JSON schema, the reliable structured-output pattern. Prompt-only JSON is less reliable, and raising temperature increases variability. Regex-scraping fenced JSON is the brittleness tool use avoids. Omitting the system prompt does not induce structured output.
referenceText: Tool use with a defined input_schema and tool_choice can force Claude to produce structured JSON output matching your schema.

stem: Claude keeps generating commit messages in an inconsistent style despite a written description of the desired format. The engineer wants the most effective prompt-engineering technique to lock in the style. What should they add?
options: A firm instruction that Claude must never deviate from the format under any circumstances.
Several concrete input/output examples demonstrating the exact commit-message format you want.
A higher max_tokens value so Claude has room to self-correct the format.
A request that Claude explain its formatting choices after each message.
rationale: Multishot (few-shot) examples are the highest-leverage way to pin down an exact output style, because the model imitates demonstrated patterns more reliably than described ones. Absolute never-deviate wording does not improve consistency. Raising max_tokens affects length budget, not format adherence. Post-hoc explanations add noise without fixing style.
referenceText: Multishot prompting: including several well-chosen examples significantly improves the consistency and accuracy of Claude's output format.

stem: An engineer is debugging a failing test with Claude Code and wants to maximize the chance Claude fixes the real root cause rather than guessing. Which two practices best support reliable debugging? (Select 2.)
options: Paste the exact failing test output and stack trace so Claude reasons from concrete evidence.
Tell Claude the cause is 'probably a typo somewhere' and ask it to skim the file quickly.
Have Claude apply a fix and commit immediately without re-running the tests to save time.
Let Claude run the test suite so it can observe the failure and verify its fix against real results.
Disable the failing test so the suite passes and the pipeline unblocks.
rationale: Giving Claude the exact error output and letting it run the tests to observe and verify grounds the fix in real behavior. Vague hints bias Claude toward guessing. Committing without re-running tests skips verification. Disabling the test hides the defect rather than resolving it.
referenceText: Effective debugging with Claude Code: provide the error/stack trace and let Claude run tests to reproduce and verify fixes.

stem: After a long Claude Code session spanning several unrelated tasks, responses are getting slower and less focused as the context fills. The engineer is about to start a brand-new, unrelated task. What is the most appropriate context-management action?
options: Run /compact after every message to keep the context permanently minimal.
Delete CLAUDE.md so project memory stops consuming the context window.
Run /clear to reset the conversation context before starting the unrelated task.
Lower max_tokens so each response leaves more room in the context window.
rationale: /clear resets conversation history, ideal when moving to an unrelated task so stale context stops degrading focus and speed. /compact summarizes rather than clears, and running it after every message is wasteful. Deleting CLAUDE.md discards useful guidance. Lowering max_tokens caps output length, not accumulated context.
referenceText: /clear resets the conversation context; /compact summarizes the conversation to reduce context usage.

stem: A complex task requires searching a large monorepo for all usages of a deprecated API, which would flood the main conversation with file contents and noise. The engineer wants that exploration isolated so it does not bloat the primary context. Which Claude Code feature is designed for this?
options: Raise the main session's context limit and paste in every file so nothing is missed.
Delegate the search to a subagent, which runs in its own context and returns only a concise result to the main thread.
Switch to headless mode mid-session so intermediate output is discarded automatically.
Run /init to regenerate CLAUDE.md so the deprecated API is documented instead of searched.
rationale: Subagents run with their own isolated context and report back a summarized result, keeping heavy exploration from polluting the main conversation. Pasting every file does the opposite. Headless mode is a non-interactive run configuration, not a mid-session isolation switch. /init bootstraps memory and does not perform or isolate the search.
referenceText: Subagents operate in a separate context window and return results to the main agent, preserving the primary conversation's context.

stem: A team wants a linter to run automatically every time Claude Code edits a file, deterministically and without relying on Claude to remember to do it. Which Claude Code mechanism enforces this?
options: Add 'always run the linter after edits' to CLAUDE.md and rely on Claude to comply each time.
Create a slash command that the engineer invokes after every change.
Set --output-format json so lint results are appended to each response automatically.
Configure a PostToolUse hook that runs the linter after file-editing tools execute.
rationale: Hooks are shell commands the harness runs deterministically at lifecycle points; a PostToolUse hook fires after tool execution (e.g., edits) and can run a linter regardless of what Claude decides. CLAUDE.md instructions rely on the model choosing to comply. A manual slash command depends on the human remembering. Output format controls serialization and runs no linter.
referenceText: Hooks are user-defined shell commands that execute deterministically at lifecycle events such as PostToolUse, e.g., to run formatters or linters after edits.

stem: A team keeps re-typing the same instructions to Claude Code every session: use pnpm not npm, run tests with `pnpm test`, and never edit files under `generated/`. They want Claude to automatically honor these rules in every session for this repository without pasting them each time. What is the most appropriate mechanism?
options: Commit a CLAUDE.md at the repository root describing the conventions, so it is auto-loaded into context each session.
Supply the rules via the --append-system-prompt flag on every invocation.
Create a /rules slash command that prints the conventions when someone runs it.
Add the rules as comments at the top of each source file so Claude reads them when it opens files.
rationale: CLAUDE.md at the project root is automatically pulled into context at the start of every session and is the canonical place for persistent conventions; committing it shares it with the team. --append-system-prompt must be supplied each run. A /rules command only helps if someone invokes it. Per-file comments are read only when that file is opened and do not establish repo-wide rules.
referenceText: Claude Code memory: CLAUDE.md is loaded automatically at session start and is the recommended place for project conventions.

stem: An engineer wants Claude Code to research a large refactor, propose a step-by-step approach, and get their sign-off BEFORE any files are modified. Which Claude Code capability directly enforces this read-only investigate-then-approve workflow?
options: Running Claude Code with the --dangerously-skip-permissions flag.
Adding a CLAUDE.md line stating 'always ask before editing.'
Using a /commit slash command to gate changes behind version control.
Plan mode, which restricts Claude to read-only analysis and presents a plan for approval before execution.
rationale: Plan mode constrains Claude to read/analyze without making edits, then surfaces a plan the user must approve before execution. --dangerously-skip-permissions does the opposite by removing prompts. A CLAUDE.md instruction is a soft guideline, not a hard gate. A /commit command runs after changes are already made, so it cannot prevent premature edits.
referenceText: Claude Code common workflows: plan mode lets Claude investigate and propose a plan before making changes.

stem: A developer creates a file at `.claude/commands/review-pr.md` containing the line 'Review the diff and focus on $ARGUMENTS'. In a session they type `/review-pr security`. What happens?
options: Claude opens a file named security in the repo and appends its contents to the prompt before running the command
Claude reports that slash commands cannot accept arguments and runs the template with $ARGUMENTS left literal
Claude runs the review-pr command with 'security' substituted for $ARGUMENTS in the prompt template
Claude ignores the file because the command must first be registered under a commands key in settings.json
rationale: A Markdown file in .claude/commands defines a project-scoped custom slash command; the filename becomes the command name and $ARGUMENTS is replaced by the text following the invocation, so 'security' is substituted into the prompt. The argument is a literal string, not a filename to open. Slash commands support arguments via $ARGUMENTS. Commands are defined by these Markdown files, not registered in settings.json.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands: custom commands live in .claude/commands as Markdown files and support the $ARGUMENTS placeholder.

stem: A CI pipeline needs Claude Code to run non-interactively on each pull request, emit a fixed-format JSON summary of lint findings, and exit so a downstream script can parse the result. Which invocation approach best fits?
options: Run Claude Code in headless mode with the -p (print) flag, requesting JSON output for the downstream parser
Launch the interactive TUI in the runner and capture the rendered terminal buffer for parsing
Use plan mode so the pipeline can approve each proposed step before it runs
Start an interactive session, pipe the diff to stdin, and read the prose replies line by line
rationale: Headless mode (`claude -p`) runs a single non-interactive prompt, can emit structured JSON suited for parsing, then exits, which matches CI. The interactive TUI is built for humans and is not reliably scrapable. Plan mode needs an interactive approver that does not exist in an unattended pipeline. Reading free-form prose from an interactive session is brittle and not fixed-format.
referenceText: Claude Code headless mode: use -p for non-interactive runs and structured output in automation and CI.

stem: A team wants their CLAUDE.md to steer Claude Code effectively across many sessions. Which practices improve its usefulness? (Select 2.)
options: Keep it concise and specific, listing the concrete build/test commands and code conventions Claude should follow
Keep it current, documenting the important project context Claude actually needs and pruning stale entries
Embed the full source of the core modules so Claude never has to open those files on demand
Commit API keys and tokens in it so Claude can authenticate to internal services during a session
Paste the latest full test-suite output each release so Claude always knows the current pass/fail state
rationale: CLAUDE.md works best when concise and specific about the commands and conventions Claude needs, and when kept current so stale guidance does not mislead. Every token loads each session, so embedding full module source wastes the window and duplicates what Claude can read on demand. Committing secrets is a serious security risk. Pasting transient test output bloats memory with data that is stale by the next run.
referenceText: Claude Code memory best practices: keep CLAUDE.md concise, specific, and current; do not store secrets.

stem: A monorepo has a root CLAUDE.md with general rules and a `services/payments/CLAUDE.md` with payments-specific rules. Claude Code is editing a file inside `services/payments/`. How are these memory files applied?
options: Only the root CLAUDE.md applies; a nested file is read only when no root file exists
Both load, with the more specific services/payments/CLAUDE.md layered on top of the root file
Only services/payments/CLAUDE.md applies; the root file is dropped once a nested file is present
The nested file applies only if the root CLAUDE.md explicitly imports it with an @path reference
rationale: Claude Code uses hierarchical memory: it loads the root CLAUDE.md plus more specific CLAUDE.md files closer to the working files, layering the specific context over the general one. The root is not ignored, nor is it wholly replaced by a nested file. Nested files are discovered automatically based on the working directory; they do not require an explicit @import from the root.
referenceText: Claude Code memory: supports project-level and nested CLAUDE.md files that combine hierarchically.

stem: A developer wants Claude Code to read details from their company's internal ticketing system while coding. The vendor publishes an MCP server. What is the correct integration path?
options: Register the vendor's MCP server in Claude Code's MCP configuration so its tools and resources are available to Claude
Wrap the ticketing REST API as a set of custom slash commands under .claude/commands for Claude to call
Export tickets to a local file each morning and reference that file from CLAUDE.md so Claude reads it at startup
Add the ticketing API base URL to settings.json so Claude Code proxies requests to it automatically
rationale: MCP is the standard for connecting Claude Code to external tools and data; registering the vendor's MCP server exposes its tools and resources to Claude for live reads. Slash commands are prompt templates, not a live-data protocol. A daily file export is stale and manual. settings.json has no facility to turn an arbitrary API base URL into an integrated data source.
referenceText: Claude Code MCP: connect external tools and data sources by configuring MCP servers.

stem: During a long debugging session Claude Code's context is filling with the output of many exploratory commands, and responses are drifting from the original goal. The user wants to preserve the essential findings while freeing context. Which action best addresses this?
options: Run /clear to reset the context, then manually restate every finding from memory
Use /compact to summarize and condense the conversation while retaining the key findings
Spawn a subagent for each remaining command so their output never enters the main context
Switch to a larger-context model mid-session so the accumulated output no longer matters
rationale: /compact condenses the transcript into a summary, reclaiming window space while retaining important state and findings, which is exactly the need. /clear discards everything, including the findings the user wants to keep. Subagents help with new work but do not condense the context already accumulated. Switching models does not remove the transcript already in the window and interrupts continuity.
referenceText: Claude Code: /compact summarizes the conversation to reduce context usage while preserving key information.

stem: A staff engineer wants Claude Code to run `pnpm lint --fix` and block the edit if linting fails every time Claude writes a file, without relying on Claude choosing to do so. Which feature is designed for this deterministic automation?
options: Configure a PostToolUse hook that runs the linter after file-editing tools and blocks on failure
Add a firm instruction to CLAUDE.md telling Claude to run the linter after every edit
Define a /lint slash command and rely on the user to run it after each change
Add the linter to the permissions allowlist so it is invoked automatically on writes
rationale: Hooks are shell commands the harness runs deterministically at lifecycle events; a PostToolUse hook fires after file-editing tools and can enforce linting regardless of the model's choices. CLAUDE.md is a soft instruction the model may skip. A manual slash command depends on the user remembering. The permissions allowlist governs whether a command may run, not automatic post-edit execution.
referenceText: Claude Code hooks: shell commands run deterministically at events such as PostToolUse.

stem: A user asks Claude Code to add a feature that requires touching 30 files across an unfamiliar codebase. To reduce the risk of Claude going off-track and keep the work reviewable, what is the recommended approach?
options: Have Claude draft a plan in plan mode, review and refine it, then execute in reviewable increments
Ask Claude to complete all 30 edits in one uninterrupted turn to maximize internal consistency
Run with --dangerously-skip-permissions so the large change is not interrupted by prompts
Have Claude make every edit on a detached branch with no intermediate commits until it compiles
rationale: For large, unfamiliar changes, using plan mode to align on the approach and then executing in reviewable increments keeps work on track and easy to review. A single uninterrupted turn removes checkpoints and makes errors harder to catch. Skipping permissions raises risk on a broad change. Deferring all commits until it compiles hides the incremental history reviewers rely on.
referenceText: Claude Code common workflows: use plan mode for complex changes and work in reviewable steps.

stem: A developer wants a reusable slash command that every teammate gets automatically when they clone the repo, versus one that exists only on their own machine across all projects. Which statement correctly distinguishes the two scopes?
options: Project commands live in the repo's .claude/commands/ and ship via git; personal commands live in ~/.claude/commands/ and apply across all of the user's projects
Project commands live in ~/.claude/commands/; personal commands live in the repo's .claude/commands/ directory
Both scopes are declared under a commands key in settings.json, so the file location does not affect sharing
Project commands ship via git; personal commands must be recreated per project because there is no user-level command directory
rationale: Project-scoped commands live in the repo's .claude/commands/ and travel through git, while personal (user) commands live under ~/.claude/commands/ and apply across all of that user's projects. The two directories are not swapped. Commands are Markdown files, not settings.json entries. A user-level command directory does exist, so personal commands need not be recreated per project.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands: project commands in .claude/commands are shared via git; personal commands in ~/.claude/commands apply across projects.

stem: Claude Code is asked to fix a failing test, but the failure is caused by a flaky external network call that timed out on the first run. What is the most reliable way for the user to help Claude handle this correctly?
options: Share the actual error output and ask Claude to distinguish a genuine defect from a flaky/environmental failure before editing code
Instruct Claude to re-run the test in a loop until it passes, then stop
Ask Claude to remove the failing assertion so the suite reports green
Have Claude wrap the test body in a broad try/except that swallows the timeout
rationale: Giving Claude the real error output and asking it to classify the failure lets it choose an appropriate fix (a retry or a mock) rather than corrupting logic. Re-running until green hides nondeterminism without fixing it. Removing the assertion deletes coverage and masks the bug. Swallowing the exception silences a real signal and can hide future genuine failures.
referenceText: Claude Code debugging workflows: provide error output and let Claude diagnose root cause before editing.

stem: A platform team is building a headless Claude Code job for CI that must be robust and safe. Which practices are appropriate for this automated, non-interactive context? (Select 2.)
options: Scope tool permissions with an allowlist so the job can run only the commands it needs
Capture and parse the structured JSON output to drive the downstream pipeline steps
Enable an interactive approval prompt so a human confirms each action during the run
Pass --dangerously-skip-permissions and grant unrestricted shell access to avoid failures
Set a high retry count so the job keeps re-running the same prompt until it exits zero
rationale: In CI, constraining the permission allowlist limits blast radius, and consuming structured JSON output lets later stages act deterministically. An interactive approval prompt cannot be satisfied in an unattended pipeline and would hang the job. Broadly skipping permissions with unrestricted shell access is a major security risk. Retrying until exit zero masks real failures rather than surfacing them.
referenceText: Claude Code headless/CI: use permission allowlists and structured output; avoid interactive prompts in automation.

stem: A user has iterated in an interactive session that has drifted far from the original request and accumulated dead ends. They want to hand the task to a fresh context in the same project. Which command does this?
options: /compact, which summarizes the transcript but keeps the drift and dead ends in condensed form
/clear, which resets the conversation context for a fresh start in the same project
/init, which regenerates the project's CLAUDE.md and begins a new conversation
/resume, which reloads a previous session's full context into the current one
rationale: /clear resets the conversation context for a clean slate while staying in the same project, which is what the user wants after accumulating dead ends. /compact only summarizes, so drift and clutter persist in condensed form. /init scaffolds a CLAUDE.md, not a conversation reset. /resume reloads prior context, reintroducing the clutter the user is escaping.
referenceText: Claude Code CLI: /clear resets the current conversation context.

stem: A documentation task requires Claude Code to summarize dozens of modules and then write a cohesive architecture overview. To keep the main session focused and avoid polluting its context with every file's raw contents, what is the recommended pattern?
options: Delegate per-module summarization to subagents whose detailed context stays isolated, returning only concise summaries to the main session
Load every module's full source into the main context at once so nothing is missed
Summarize modules one at a time in the main session, running /compact after each to reclaim space
Store each module's full text in CLAUDE.md so it stays in memory for the whole task
rationale: Subagents run in their own context windows, so delegating per-module summarization keeps bulky raw reading isolated and returns only distilled summaries to the orchestrating session. Loading all sources at once risks overflow and dilution. Repeated /compact loses fidelity and still routes every raw file through the main context first. Full module text in CLAUDE.md permanently bloats every session's memory.
referenceText: Claude Code subagents: run in separate context windows and return focused results to the main session.

stem: A team wants to embed Claude's agentic coding loop inside their own internal web application with a custom UI and orchestration. Which approach is most appropriate?
options: Use the Claude Agent SDK, which exposes the same agent harness (tools, context management, the loop) for building custom agent applications
Embed the interactive Claude Code TUI in an iframe and treat it as the application's backend
Shell out to the Claude Code CLI in headless mode for every user action from the web server
Call the Messages API directly and rebuild tool orchestration and context management from scratch
rationale: The Claude Agent SDK provides the same agent harness that powers Claude Code (tool use, context management, the agentic loop) as building blocks for embedding agents in custom applications. The human-facing TUI is not a supported programmatic backend. Shelling out to the CLI per action is fragile and not built for embedded UI/orchestration. Calling the Messages API raw discards the ready-made harness the SDK provides.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK: build custom agents on the same harness that powers Claude Code.

stem: A refactoring task asks Claude Code to rename a widely used function and update all call sites. Partway through, Claude realizes the change would also break a public API that external consumers depend on. According to reliability best practices, what is the appropriate behavior?
options: Pause and escalate to the user, surfacing the public-API impact and asking how to proceed before changing the contract
Rename the public API as well and continue, since the task said to update all call sites
Silently keep the public signature but add an internal alias and migrate the rest without noting it
Stop work and revert the completed renames without reporting why
rationale: When a change exceeds the safe scope of the request, such as altering a public API contract, the reliable behavior is to pause and escalate with the impact and options so a human decides. Silently renaming the public API breaks external consumers. Adding an alias silently still changes behavior without the user's knowledge and hides a scope decision. Reverting without explanation strands the user with no path forward.
referenceText: Claude agent reliability: escalate to a human when an action exceeds intended scope or has broad impact.

stem: A developer wants Claude Code, run headless in a script, to return JSON that always conforms to a fixed shape (fields: summary, files_changed, risk_level) so the wrapper never parses free-form prose. Beyond requesting JSON output, which prompt-engineering practice most improves conformance?
options: State the exact schema and include a concrete example of the desired JSON in the prompt
Request the answer as Markdown and extract the fields downstream with regex
Ask the model to include any additional fields it judges relevant so the output is comprehensive
Instruct the model to prepend a natural-language explanation before the JSON block
rationale: Explicitly stating the required schema and giving a concrete example (few-shot) strongly steers the model toward consistent, machine-parseable output. Markdown plus regex scraping is brittle and the opposite of structured output. Allowing extra fields guarantees schema drift. Prepending prose forces the wrapper to strip narrative text and risks breaking parsing.
referenceText: Prompt engineering for structured output: specify the schema and include an example to increase JSON conformance.

stem: A team wants a custom slash command `/fix-issue` that takes an issue number as an argument, reads the issue via their GitHub MCP tools, and drafts a fix. Where should the reusable command definition live, and what should it reference?
options: A Markdown file at .claude/commands/fix-issue.md whose prompt uses $ARGUMENTS and instructs Claude to use the configured GitHub MCP tools
A commands entry in settings.json that hardcodes the issue number and the fix template
A PostToolUse hook that fires after each edit to fetch the issue and rewrite the code
A CLAUDE.md section describing the fix steps, invoked when the user pastes the issue body
rationale: Custom commands are Markdown prompt templates in .claude/commands; the file can reference $ARGUMENTS for the issue number and instruct Claude to use whatever MCP tools are configured, such as GitHub, to read the issue. Commands are not JSON in settings.json, and hardcoding a number defeats reuse. A PostToolUse hook fires on edits, not on-demand invocation. A CLAUDE.md note is not an invokable command and would require manual pasting.
referenceText: Claude Code slash commands: Markdown templates that can invoke available tools and take $ARGUMENTS.

stem: A user notices Claude Code repeatedly asks for permission before running `git status` and `pnpm test`, which are safe and frequent in their workflow. They want to stop the prompts for just these commands without disabling permissions entirely. What is the correct approach?
options: Add those specific commands to the permissions allowlist in the project's .claude/settings.json
Launch every session with --dangerously-skip-permissions to remove all prompts
List the commands in CLAUDE.md so Claude treats them as pre-approved and skips the prompt
Wrap the commands inside a custom slash command, which the permission system does not gate
rationale: The permissions configuration in settings.json lets you allowlist specific commands so they run without prompting while everything else still requires approval, matching the request precisely. --dangerously-skip-permissions removes all safety prompts, far broader than intended. CLAUDE.md is memory, not a permissions grant. Slash commands still run tools through the permission model, so wrapping does not and should not bypass it.
referenceText: Claude Code settings: configure a permissions allowlist to pre-approve specific tools/commands.

stem: In a multi-agent research system, the coordinator frequently performs web research itself in the main thread instead of delegating to the web-search subagent. Which change most directly improves delegation?
options: Write a clear, action-oriented description on the web-search subagent stating exactly when it should be used
Grant the web-search subagent access to every tool the coordinator can use
Remove the web-search tool from the coordinator so it has no ability to research directly
Lower the coordinator model's temperature so it delegates more decisively
rationale: Claude routes to a subagent by matching the task to the subagent's description; a vague description leaves the coordinator doing the work itself, so a clear, action-oriented description most directly improves delegation. Broad tool access does not change routing. Stripping the coordinator's tool is a blunt workaround that can break legitimate direct use and still does not teach the model when to delegate. Temperature affects sampling, not delegation logic.
referenceText: Claude Code subagents: Claude uses each subagent's description to decide when to delegate.

stem: A document-analysis stage must process 40 large PDFs. Why is routing this to a dedicated subagent preferable to analyzing them directly in the coordinator's conversation?
options: Each subagent runs in its own context window and returns only a distilled summary, keeping the coordinator's context uncluttered
A subagent can process documents that individually exceed the model's maximum context window
Subagent token usage is not counted against the coordinator's budget or billed
Subagents run all 40 analyses in true parallel so the coordinator never waits
rationale: Subagents preserve the coordinator's context by doing bulky reading in a separate window and returning only the summary. Each subagent still has a finite context window, so it cannot process a document that exceeds that limit. Billing is token-based, not exempt. While work can be distributed, the benefit here is context isolation, not a guarantee of fully parallel, wait-free execution.
referenceText: Subagents: each runs in its own context window and returns only the summary.

stem: In a research pipeline, the web-search subagent issues a high volume of short, low-complexity fetch-and-extract calls that now dominate spend, while the coordinator does complex planning. You want to cut cost without changing how the coordinator reasons. What is the most appropriate change?
options: Cap the coordinator's `max_tokens` at a low value so it produces less output
Set the web-search subagent's `model` to a faster, cheaper model such as Haiku
Move the subagent's definition into the project CLAUDE.md so it loads once per session
Strip the subagent's tools so it stops making outbound API calls
rationale: A subagent can declare its own `model`, so routing the high-volume, low-complexity search work to Haiku reduces cost while the coordinator keeps its stronger model. Capping the coordinator's max_tokens truncates its output rather than the subagent's cost driver. Relocating the definition into CLAUDE.md does not change model selection. Removing tools disables the subagent entirely.
referenceText: Subagents can be configured to use a specific model, so high-volume, low-complexity work can be routed to faster, cheaper models like Haiku.

stem: A team repeatedly pastes the same multi-step research procedure into Claude Code and only changes the topic each time. What is the best way to package it as a reusable, parameterized command?
options: Create a custom slash command as a Markdown file with frontmatter, using `$ARGUMENTS` for the topic
Add the procedure as a commit-message template so it appears on each commit
Save the procedure in a code comment at the repository root for reference
Raise the coordinator's `max_tokens` so it can hold all the steps at once
rationale: Custom slash commands package a repeatable prompt into an invokable command defined in Markdown with frontmatter and `$ARGUMENTS` placeholders for parameters like the topic. Commit templates and code comments are never executed by Claude Code as commands, and max_tokens controls output length, not reusability.
referenceText: Claude Code custom slash commands are defined as Markdown files and support argument placeholders such as $ARGUMENTS.

stem: Every subagent in the project (search, analysis, synthesis, report) must cite sources using the same numbered-footnote house style. Where should this convention live so all agents pick it up automatically for the whole session?
options: In each developer's shell profile so it is exported into the environment
In a top-level `.gitignore` entry that all agents read at startup
In CLAUDE.md at the project root
Only in the final report template, applied after generation finishes
rationale: CLAUDE.md is loaded as project memory and shapes agent behavior across the whole session, making it the right home for a shared citation convention. Shell profiles and .gitignore are not read as model instructions, and applying the style only after generation does not guide the subagents that actually produce the citations.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md is loaded automatically as project memory and provides shared instructions to Claude across the session.

stem: You need a nightly CI job to run the research system unattended and emit machine-readable output for a downstream pipeline step to consume. Which approach fits?
options: Launch the interactive TUI and leave the session open overnight
Run Claude Code in headless mode with `-p` and `--output-format json`
Enable plan mode so the run pauses for approval before each stage
Disable all tools so the job runs deterministically without external calls
rationale: Headless mode (`-p`) is built for non-interactive and CI use, and `--output-format json` produces structured output for downstream steps to parse. The interactive TUI and plan mode both require a human in the loop, and disabling tools would prevent the research work from happening at all.
referenceText: Headless mode runs Claude Code non-interactively with the -p flag and supports --output-format json for programmatic consumption.

stem: The web-search subagent must call a live search API that is exposed as an external tool. In the Claude Agent SDK, what is the idiomatic way to make that capability available to the agent?
options: Paste representative search results into the system prompt before each run
Store the API key in CLAUDE.md so the model can reach the network directly
Fine-tune the model on a corpus of historical search results
Configure an MCP server in `mcpServers` so its tools become available to the agent
rationale: MCP servers configured through `mcpServers` expose tools the agent can invoke at runtime, which is the intended integration path in the Agent SDK. Static pasted results are not live search, a model cannot make network calls merely because a key sits in CLAUDE.md, and fine-tuning changes weights without granting live tool access.
referenceText: The Agent SDK integrates external tools by configuring MCP servers via the mcpServers option, whose tools the agent can then call.

stem: The report-generation subagent must emit an object with `title`, `sections[]`, and `citations[]` that a downstream service parses programmatically. Which approach most reliably guarantees the output is schema-valid?
options: Use structured outputs (json_schema output format) with `required` fields and `additionalProperties: false`
Ask the model in the prompt for "valid JSON" and repair the result with a regex fallback
Raise the temperature so the model explores more JSON shapes and picks a valid one
Return Markdown and let the downstream service infer the fields from headings
rationale: Structured outputs constrain generation to a JSON schema, enforcing the required fields and rejecting stray keys via additionalProperties:false. Prompt-plus-regex is brittle and unguaranteed, higher temperature increases variability rather than reducing it, and Markdown forces fragile downstream parsing.
referenceText: Structured outputs use a json_schema output format to constrain responses to required fields and forbid extra keys.

stem: During a turn, the web-search subagent emits a tool_use block. How should the application continue the conversation correctly?
options: Return the tool output as a new system prompt on the next request
Start a fresh conversation with no prior messages to keep the context small
Execute the tool and send its output back as a `tool_result` block referencing the `tool_use` id
Ignore the tool call and instruct the model to answer from its own knowledge
rationale: The tool-use loop requires returning a `tool_result` that references the originating `tool_use` id so the model can consume the output and continue the turn. Tool output is not a system prompt, restarting the conversation discards state, and ignoring the call defeats tool use and invites hallucination.
referenceText: Tool results must be returned as tool_result content blocks that reference the corresponding tool_use id.

stem: The synthesis subagent formats citations inconsistently from run to run. Which prompt technique most reliably locks in a single target format?
options: Append the instruction "be consistent about formatting" to the end of the prompt
Provide a few worked examples showing sources in and the exact citation format out
Increase `max_tokens` so the model has more room to format the citations
Remove all examples so conflicting styles cannot confuse the model
rationale: Few-shot (multishot) examples demonstrate the exact target format and are the most reliable way to standardize output style. A vague 'be consistent' instruction supplies no concrete target, max_tokens governs length rather than format, and removing examples discards the strongest available steering signal.
referenceText: Multishot prompting with worked examples reliably steers the model's output format.

stem: The document-analysis subagent occasionally follows text embedded inside a source PDF as though it were a task instruction. What is the best in-prompt mitigation?
options: Wrap each source in clearly named XML tags (e.g., <source>...</source>) to separate data from instructions
Concatenate all sources into one unlabeled block so the model reads them uniformly
Interleave source text and task instructions line by line for tighter coupling
Strip all delimiters so the model reads everything as continuous prose
rationale: XML tags create explicit structure that lets the model distinguish untrusted source data from your instructions, reducing prompt-injection risk. Unlabeled concatenation and line-by-line interleaving blur that boundary, and removing delimiters eliminates it entirely.
referenceText: XML tags help structure prompts and clearly separate source data from instructions.
