Comment on every line of the diff so nothing is missed, even where there is no issue
Prioritize findings that make the review look the most thorough
rationale: Citing file/line with a concrete fix, deferring to existing linters, and requiring justification-or-low-confidence all produce actionable feedback while minimizing false positives. Commenting on every line is pure noise. Prioritizing findings by how thorough they look encourages nitpicking rather than signal.
referenceText: Prompt engineering: be clear, direct, and constrain output for actionable feedback.

stem: The review workflow authenticates to the Anthropic API using an API key. A teammate suggests committing the key to CLAUDE.md 'so every run picks it up automatically.' What is the correct guidance?
options: Commit it to CLAUDE.md but keep the repository private so it is safe
Store the key as a CI secret / environment variable injected at runtime, never committed to the repo
Paste the key into a slash command file since those stay local to the checkout
Hard-code it in the workflow YAML and rely on branch protection
rationale: Secrets must be injected at runtime via the CI secret store / environment variables and kept out of version control. Committing to CLAUDE.md (even in a private repo) exposes the key to everyone with repo access and to history. Slash command files are committed too. Hard-coding in YAML commits the secret regardless of branch protection.
referenceText: Claude Code CI/GitHub Actions setup: provide the API key via secrets.

stem: The parser occasionally receives valid JSON from Claude but with a 'severity' value like 'kinda important' that your code doesn't recognize, causing a crash. Beyond validating in code, which schema-side change most reduces this class of error?
options: Ask Claude to 'use standard severities' in plain English within the prompt
Constrain severity to an enum (blocker | major | minor | info) in the tool's input schema
Keep severity as a free-text string and normalize it later with fuzzy matching
Remove the severity field entirely and infer it from the length of the message
rationale: Declaring severity as an enum in the tool's input schema restricts the model to allowed values at generation time, preventing out-of-vocabulary strings. A plain-English request is not enforced by the schema. Free text plus fuzzy matching reintroduces ambiguity. Inferring severity from message length is arbitrary and unreliable.
referenceText: Tool use: JSON Schema enums constrain allowed field values.

stem: During a review run, the GitHub MCP tool call to post a comment returns a rate-limit (HTTP 429) error. The current code ignores tool errors, so the run reports success while comments silently fail to post. What is the best-designed handling?
options: Swallow the error and mark the review passed so the pipeline stays green
Surface the tool error: retry with backoff, and if it still fails, fail the job or escalate so the failure is visible
Immediately retry the same call in a tight loop with no delay until it succeeds
Switch to posting the comment by scraping the GitHub web UI instead
rationale: Rate-limit errors should be handled with retry-and-backoff and, if unresolved, surfaced as a job failure or escalation so the problem is visible and recoverable. Swallowing the error hides real failures behind a green build. Tight-loop retries worsen rate limiting. Falling back to UI scraping is brittle and unsupported.
referenceText: Tool use: handle tool errors and return instructive retry guidance.

stem: A stakeholder asks you to enable plan mode in the headless CI review so the bot 'thinks before it writes.' You need to evaluate this suggestion. What is the accurate assessment?
options: Plan mode is required for headless CI and the job cannot run without it
Plan mode is designed for interactive, read-only planning with human approval; in a non-interactive CI gate you instead express the desired approach in the prompt and keep the job automated
Plan mode makes the model faster by skipping tool calls in CI
Plan mode disables CLAUDE.md so reviews become more objective
rationale: Plan mode is an interactive, read-only mode meant for a human to review and approve a plan before execution, which does not fit an unattended CI gate; the equivalent 'think first' behavior is achieved through prompt design while keeping the job automated. It is not required for headless runs, does not speed anything up by skipping tools, and does not disable CLAUDE.md.
referenceText: Claude Code: plan mode is interactive, read-only planning.

stem: To keep the CI reviewer least-privilege and predictable, which controls should you configure for the headless run? (Select 2.)
options: Use --allowedTools (or equivalent permission config) to whitelist only the tools the review needs
Run in non-interactive mode so it never blocks waiting for a permission prompt no human will answer
Grant write access to the production deployment tools in case the reviewer wants to hotfix
Disable all output formatting so nothing can be parsed and misused
rationale: Whitelisting tools and running non-interactively are the correct guardrails for an unattended job: they bound capability and prevent hangs on unanswered prompts. Granting deploy write access to a reviewer violates least privilege and scope. Disabling output formatting breaks the machine-readable result the pipeline depends on.
referenceText: Claude Code CLI: allowedTools and non-interactive permission handling.

stem: An extraction pipeline pulls fields from scanned invoices into a fixed JSON shape. The current prompt asks Claude in prose to 'reply with only JSON,' but responses intermittently include a markdown code fence or a sentence of preamble that breaks the downstream parser. Which change most reliably yields machine-parseable, schema-conforming output?
options: Raise temperature so Claude explores cleaner formatting
Append 'Do NOT include any other text' several times at the end of the prompt
Define a tool whose input_schema is the target JSON schema, force it with tool_choice, and read the structured object from the tool_use.input block
Post-process every response with a regex that strips code fences and leading prose
rationale: Tool use is the sanctioned mechanism for structured output: Claude returns the extracted data as a validated tool_use.input object matching your input_schema, eliminating prose and fences. Temperature does not govern envelope formatting. Repeating negative instructions is brittle and easily violated. Regex post-processing patches symptoms but still fails on novel wrappers and does not enforce the schema.
referenceText: Tool use overview — structured/JSON output via tools.

stem: You have exactly one extraction tool named extract_invoice and you want Claude to call it on every request rather than answering in prose. Which tool_choice value forces Claude to invoke that specific named tool?
options: {"type": "auto"}
{"type": "tool", "name": "extract_invoice"}
{"type": "any"}
{"type": "none"}
rationale: {"type":"tool","name":...} forces Claude to call that named tool. 'auto' lets Claude decide whether to use any tool, so it may answer in text. 'any' forces some tool call but lets the model pick which one, so it does not name a specific tool. 'none' forbids tool calls entirely.
referenceText: Implement tool use — forcing tool use with tool_choice.

stem: Some invoices omit a purchase-order number entirely, and testers report Claude sometimes invents a plausible-looking PO number when the field is absent. What is the best schema-and-prompt design to handle this edge case?
options: Mark po_number as required and lower temperature to 0 so Claude stops guessing
Remove po_number from the schema so a missing value cannot cause an error
Instruct Claude to fill po_number with the sentinel "UNKNOWN" formatted like a real PO number
Make po_number nullable in the schema and instruct Claude to return null when the value is not present in the document
rationale: Making the field nullable and explicitly instructing Claude to emit null for absent values lets it distinguish 'not in the document' from a real value, the core of graceful edge-case handling and hallucination reduction. Temperature 0 reduces variance but a required field can still be fabricated. Dropping the field loses data you may need. A realistic sentinel is indistinguishable downstream from a genuine value.
referenceText: Reduce hallucinations — allow Claude to say it doesn't know

stem: Invoice dates appear as '3/4/25', '04-Mar-2025', and 'March 4, 2025', but the downstream system requires ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DD). Extraction accuracy on dates is inconsistent. Which prompt-engineering technique most directly improves normalization?
options: Increase max_tokens so Claude has more room to format the date
Tell Claude to 'be careful with dates' without providing examples
Provide a few-shot set of input snippets paired with the exact normalized ISO output for each ambiguous format
Ask Claude to output the date in whatever format appears in the document
rationale: Few-shot (multishot) examples that pair messy inputs with the exact desired ISO outputs are the most effective way to pin down an ambiguous transformation like date normalization. More tokens does not teach the target format. A vague admonition gives no concrete pattern. Echoing the source format defeats the normalization requirement.
referenceText: Multishot prompting (few-shot examples)

stem: After Claude returns extracted JSON, your service validates it against the canonical JSON Schema with a validator library. Occasionally a field violates the schema (e.g., a negative quantity). What is the most reliable pattern for these failures?
options: Trust the model output and disable validation to avoid blocking the pipeline
Programmatically validate in code and, on failure, re-prompt Claude with the specific validation error and the offending output so it can correct it
Silently drop any record that fails validation
Re-run the request at a lower temperature and accept whatever comes back
rationale: Deterministic, code-side schema validation is the reliable gate; feeding the concrete validation error back to Claude for a targeted correction closes the loop and is a standard structured-output reliability practice. Disabling validation ships bad data. Silently dropping records loses information without visibility. Blindly lowering temperature and re-running does not tell the model what was wrong.
referenceText: Increase output consistency — validate and iterate

stem: Extraction accuracy drops on invoices where the correct line-item total requires reconciling subtotals, discounts, and tax. Forcing a pure-JSON tool call gives Claude no room to reason and errors increase. What is the best way to preserve reasoning while still getting clean structured output?
options: Add a free-text 'reasoning' string as a required field inside the extraction schema and parse around it downstream
Remove all complex computed fields so no reasoning is ever needed
Instruct Claude to compute silently and never show any intermediate steps
Let Claude reason first (via extended thinking or a scratchpad step) and then emit the final answer through the extraction tool, keeping reasoning out of the schema fields
rationale: Chain-of-thought/extended thinking improves accuracy on multi-step reconciliation; the pattern is to reason before producing the final structured payload so the JSON stays clean and reasoning does not pollute the schema. Stuffing free-form reasoning into a schema field couples brittle prose to your data contract. Removing needed fields sacrifices requirements. Forbidding reasoning reintroduces the accuracy problem.
referenceText: Chain-of-thought / extended thinking before structured output

stem: Business stakeholders require that low-certainty extractions never post automatically to the ERP. How should you design the pipeline so uncertain results are handled gracefully rather than silently accepted?
options: Post everything to the ERP and rely on accountants to catch mistakes later
Retry uncertain documents at temperature 0 until Claude produces any answer, then post it
Include a per-record confidence signal in the tool output and route records below a threshold to a human-review queue instead of auto-posting
Reject and delete any document Claude is unsure about
rationale: Emitting a structured confidence signal and escalating sub-threshold records to human review is the canonical graceful-degradation/escalation pattern for high-stakes downstream integration. Auto-posting pushes the cost of errors downstream. Retrying at temperature 0 reduces variance but does not raise genuine confidence. Deleting uncertain documents loses legitimate records a human could resolve.
referenceText: Increase consistency — thresholds and human escalation

stem: Each request concatenates a long raw OCR dump of the invoice directly after your instructions with no delimiters, and Claude sometimes confuses instruction text with document content. What is the recommended prompt structure to prevent this?
options: Separate the instructions and the OCR text with a blank line and a row of dashes
Wrap the document body in clearly named XML tags (e.g., <document>...</document>) so Claude can unambiguously separate instructions from content
Place the instructions after the document text so the model reads the content first
Prefix the OCR text with a plain 'Document:' label but no other delimiters
rationale: XML tags are the documented way to delimit and label distinct parts of a prompt, letting Claude reliably separate instructions from the document to process. A blank line and dashes give a weak, unlabeled boundary. Reordering without labeling does not resolve the ambiguity. A bare text label without an enclosing structure still leaves the content boundary undefined.
referenceText: Use XML tags to structure prompts

stem: Some contracts run to hundreds of pages and exceed a comfortable single-call context budget, yet you must extract a structured summary of every clause. Which approach best balances completeness and reliability?
options: Truncate the document to the first N pages that fit and extract only from those
Send the whole document at once and accept whatever fits, ignoring overflow
Summarize the document into a paragraph first, then extract fields from the paragraph
Split the document into sections, extract structured data per section, then merge and de-duplicate the partial results into the final schema
rationale: Chunking by section, extracting per chunk, and merging keeps extraction complete and within context limits while preserving fidelity. Truncation and 'whatever fits' both silently drop clauses. Pre-summarizing to a paragraph destroys the granular clause-level detail the schema requires before extraction even begins.
referenceText: Context windows — managing long inputs

stem: You want to maximize the rate at which Claude returns output that conforms to your JSON schema. Which two techniques most directly and reliably improve schema conformance? (Select 2.)
options: Define the target schema as a tool input_schema and force the tool call
Raise temperature to 1.0 to increase output diversity
Provide a few-shot example showing a correctly-formatted output object
Ask Claude to 'try your best' to match the schema
Delete all property descriptions to keep the prompt short
rationale: Forcing a tool call with the schema as input_schema and supplying a concrete few-shot example are both documented, high-leverage ways to increase conformance. High temperature increases variance and hurts conformance. A vague 'try your best' gives no structural constraint. Removing property descriptions strips the guidance that helps Claude place values correctly.
referenceText: Increase output consistency (JSON) and multishot prompting

stem: Your source invoices arrive as PDFs containing both text and tables. A teammate proposes running a separate OCR tool first and pasting the raw text into the prompt, which loses table structure. What does Claude support that better preserves layout for extraction?
options: Convert the PDF to a single flattened JPEG and describe the tables in prose
Pass the PDF directly in a document content block so Claude can read text and visual layout together
Strip the PDF to plain text before sending, since Claude cannot read PDFs
Rasterize each page separately and send the pages with no table markup
rationale: Claude's PDF support lets you pass the PDF as a document block and reason over both its text and visual/tabular layout, which is more faithful than lossy pre-OCR. Flattening to one JPEG loses multi-page fidelity and text quality. It is false that Claude cannot read PDFs. Rasterizing pages without structure discards the table layout the task depends on.
referenceText: PDF support

stem: A 'payment_status' field must be exactly one of: paid, unpaid, partial, void. Claude occasionally returns near-synonyms like 'settled' or 'not paid,' breaking downstream joins. What schema construct best constrains this field?
options: Add a prose sentence asking Claude to 'please use standard statuses'
Keep payment_status as free text and normalize synonyms with a lookup table afterward only
Set payment_status to type integer and map statuses to numbers in the prompt
Define payment_status as a string with an enum of the four allowed values in the tool input_schema
rationale: An enum in the input_schema constrains the field to the exact permitted values, the direct fix for categorical drift. A prose request is not enforceable. Relying solely on post-hoc synonym mapping is brittle and always trails new variants. Encoding statuses as opaque integers loses semantics and invites off-by-one mapping errors.
referenceText: Tool use — JSON schema and input_schema constraints

stem: Re-running extraction on the same invoice sometimes yields slightly different numeric values, which complicates reconciliation and auditing downstream. Which single change most improves run-to-run stability for a deterministic extraction task?
options: Set temperature to 1 to average over more samples
Raise top_k so the model considers a wider set of candidate tokens
Set temperature to 0 so sampling is greedy and outputs are as consistent as possible for identical inputs
Increase top_p to 1.0 while leaving temperature high
rationale: For deterministic extraction where you want the most consistent output for a fixed input, temperature 0 (greedy decoding) is the right setting. Temperature 1, a higher top_k, and top_p 1.0 with high temperature all widen the sampling distribution and increase variability.
referenceText: Increase output consistency — temperature control

stem: Reviewers find Claude misclassifies which monetary value is the 'grand_total' versus the 'subtotal.' The schema currently lists both as bare number fields with no explanation. What is the most effective, low-cost fix?
options: Rename the fields to field_a and field_b so Claude is not biased by their names
Write precise natural-language descriptions on each property in the input_schema clarifying exactly what grand_total and subtotal mean and where to find them
Merge grand_total and subtotal into one field and separate the values downstream
Drop the subtotal field and derive it later by subtraction
rationale: Property-level descriptions in the schema are read by the model and are the primary lever for disambiguating semantically similar fields. Opaque names remove the very cues that guide correct placement. Merging the two fields discards the distinction you need. Dropping subtotal loses a required value and assumes a fixed arithmetic relationship that may not hold with discounts or tax.
referenceText: Tool use — write detailed tool/field descriptions

stem: During a bulk backfill your extraction service starts receiving HTTP 429 and 529 responses and some records fail. What is the correct reliability handling for these specific errors?
options: Immediately retry in a tight loop with no delay until each request succeeds
Treat 429 and 529 as permanent failures and drop those records
Lower the model's temperature so it stops returning 429 errors
Retry the failed requests with exponential backoff and jitter, respecting rate limits, rather than hammering the API immediately
rationale: 429 (rate limit) and 529 (overloaded) are transient; exponential backoff with jitter is the documented way to recover without amplifying load. Tight-loop retries worsen the overload and can extend throttling. They are not permanent failures, so dropping records loses data. Temperature is a sampling parameter unrelated to HTTP status codes.
referenceText: API errors and rate limits — handling 429/529

stem: You must extract structured data from 500,000 archived invoices overnight. Latency per document does not matter, but cost and throughput do, and results are consumed the next morning. Which Anthropic capability best fits this workload?
options: Send all 500,000 requests synchronously as fast as a single worker pool allows
Open one streaming request and place all documents into a single prompt
Submit the documents through the Message Batches API for high-volume asynchronous processing at reduced cost
Fine-tune a dedicated model on the invoices to speed up the nightly run
rationale: The Message Batches API is purpose-built for large-volume, latency-tolerant asynchronous jobs and lowers cost, ideal for an overnight backfill. Firehosing synchronous requests hits rate limits and is fragile. One giant prompt cannot hold 500k documents and destroys per-document isolation. Fine-tuning does not address batch throughput or cost for a one-off backfill and adds significant overhead.
referenceText: Message Batches API (batch processing)

stem: Every extraction request repeats the same long system prompt: detailed field descriptions, the JSON schema, and a dozen few-shot examples. Across thousands of documents this is costly and slow. Which feature reduces cost and latency for the unchanging prefix while keeping accuracy?
options: Delete the few-shot examples to shorten every prompt
Use prompt caching on the stable system prompt, schema, and examples so the shared prefix is reused across calls
Move the schema into the user message so it changes each call
Compress the schema by removing whitespace only
rationale: Prompt caching reuses a stable prefix (instructions, schema, examples) across many requests, cutting cost and latency without sacrificing the guidance that drives accuracy. Deleting examples reduces accuracy. Moving the schema into a per-call, varying position defeats caching. Whitespace stripping yields negligible savings and does not address repeated processing of the prefix.
referenceText: Prompt caching

stem: Your extraction agent must fetch source documents from an internal document store and expose them to Claude in a standardized, tool-agnostic way, so the same integration works across multiple clients. Which Model Context Protocol construct is the right fit for exposing read-only document content?
options: Inline each document's bytes in the system prompt of every request
Expose the documents as MCP tools that Claude invokes to perform actions with side effects
Rename your existing REST endpoints as MCP without implementing the protocol
Expose the documents as MCP resources served by an MCP server the client connects to
rationale: MCP resources are the standardized way for an MCP server to expose read-only content (like documents) that clients and models can consume, giving the tool-agnostic integration described. Tools model actions/side effects, not passive read-only content. Inlining bytes per request is not standardized and does not scale. Merely relabeling REST endpoints does not make them MCP-compliant.
referenceText: MCP concepts — Resources

stem: Your invoice extractor occasionally receives a document that is not an invoice at all (e.g., a cover letter). You want it to fail gracefully instead of fabricating invoice fields. Which two design choices best achieve this? (Select 2.)
options: Lower temperature so Claude extracts invoice fields more confidently from the non-invoice
Add a document_type / is_expected_document flag to the output so Claude can signal a mismatch instead of forcing extraction
Always populate every field with best-guess values to satisfy the required schema
Instruct Claude to return nulls with an explicit status when the document is not an invoice, rather than inventing values
Remove the schema entirely so any output is accepted
rationale: A document-type flag plus an instruction to emit nulls and a status when the input is off-type lets the system detect and route mismatches gracefully. Lowering temperature only makes fabricated extractions more confident. Forcing best-guess values into required fields produces convincing garbage. Dropping the schema abandons the very contract downstream systems depend on.
referenceText: Reduce hallucinations — allow abstention and flag mismatches

stem: You want a regression gate that runs your extraction over a fixed set of sample documents and asserts the outputs still validate against the JSON schema on every pull request, with no interactive terminal. Which approach fits a CI environment?
options: Require an engineer to open an interactive Claude Code session and eyeball the outputs before each merge
Skip validation in CI and only check schemas manually before releases
Run the extraction non-interactively using the Claude Agent SDK / Claude Code headless mode in the CI job and fail the build if schema validation fails
Commit the expected JSON and never re-run extraction so CI stays green
rationale: Running extraction non-interactively via the Agent SDK / Claude Code headless mode lets CI execute the pipeline and assert schema validity automatically on every PR, which is the point of a regression gate. Manual eyeballing does not scale and is not automated. Skipping CI validation defers failures to production. Freezing expected output without re-running means the gate never actually tests the extractor.
referenceText: Claude Agent SDK — headless / programmatic use

stem: Your pipeline must return a payload that always conforms to a fixed customer-record shape (name, email, account_id) so it can be inserted directly into a database. Which approach most reliably guarantees Claude emits that structure on every call?
options: Define a single tool whose input_schema is the record shape and set tool_choice to {"type":"tool","name":...} so Claude must return a tool_use block matching it
Ask Claude in the system prompt to 'respond only with JSON' and parse the assistant's free text
Add a stop sequence of '}' so generation always halts at the end of the object
Lower temperature to 0 and instruct Claude to avoid any prose
rationale: Forcing a single extraction tool via tool_choice makes Claude return a tool_use block whose input is validated against your input_schema, the canonical way to get reliable structured JSON. Prompting for JSON in prose still allows preambles, markdown fences, and drift. A '}' stop sequence truncates nested objects and breaks on the first inner brace. Temperature 0 reduces variance but does not constrain structure.
referenceText: Force a specific tool call by passing a tool with an input_schema and setting tool_choice to require that tool rather than relying on prompting.

stem: Extraction usually returns valid JSON, but about 2% of calls include an extra field or an enum value that isn't in your schema, breaking the downstream insert. You already force a single extraction tool. What is the most direct fix?
options: Add strict:true to the tool definition so tool calls are constrained to exactly match the schema
Raise temperature so Claude explores more schema-consistent completions
Increase max_tokens so Claude has more room to finish the object
Remove the enum from the schema and validate the values only in downstream code
rationale: Strict tool use (strict:true) uses grammar-constrained sampling so tool names and inputs conform exactly to the declared schema, eliminating stray fields and out-of-enum values. Higher temperature increases variance, making drift worse. More max_tokens addresses truncation, not conformance. Dropping the enum hides the problem instead of enforcing valid values. Verified against the strict tool use documentation.
referenceText: Add strict:true to custom tool definitions to enforce JSON Schema compliance on tool names and inputs via grammar-constrained sampling.

stem: Your extraction schema declares invoice_total as {"type":"number","minimum":0} and you have enabled strict structured outputs. A stakeholder concludes that a negative total is now structurally impossible and wants to drop the downstream range check. How should you respond?
options: Numeric range keywords such as minimum are not enforced by structured outputs, so the value must still be validated in application code
minimum is enforced by the grammar, so a negative total can never be returned and the check is redundant
minimum is enforced only when temperature is set to 0, so keeping the check is unnecessary in that configuration
Setting strict:true is what activates minimum enforcement, so the check can be removed once strict is on
rationale: Structured outputs enforce type, enum, required, and additionalProperties but do not enforce numeric constraints (minimum/maximum/multipleOf) or string-length constraints; those must be validated downstream. The other three options all assert enforcement the feature explicitly does not provide.
referenceText: Not supported: numerical constraints (minimum, maximum, multipleOf) and string constraints (minLength, maxLength).

stem: Some source documents genuinely have no purchase-order number. Downstream code must reliably distinguish 'this document has no PO number' from an incorrect guess. How should you design the schema and prompt?
options: Make po_number nullable and instruct Claude to return null when the field is absent from the document
Instruct Claude to infer the most likely PO number from surrounding context so the field is always populated
Omit po_number from the object entirely whenever it is missing so the payload stays minimal
Reject and route away any document that lacks a PO number before it reaches the model
rationale: A nullable field plus an explicit instruction to emit null when the value is absent lets downstream code distinguish 'not present' from a real value. Inferring a value invites hallucination; silently omitting the field makes 'missing' and 'not extracted' indistinguishable and can violate required; rejecting the document discards valid data for an expected case.
referenceText: Basic types include null; make a field nullable and instruct Claude to return null when the value is not present.

stem: Extraction accuracy on noisy scanned contracts is below target, but you cannot change the schema. Which two techniques are most likely to improve quality? (Select 2.)
options: Provide several diverse few-shot examples pairing tricky input snippets with their exact correct JSON
Let Claude reason in a scratchpad or extended-thinking step before emitting the final structured output
Raise temperature to 1.0 to increase output diversity on the extraction task
Rely on the model's parametric knowledge of typical contracts instead of grounding in the document
Shorten the prompt by removing field descriptions so the model focuses on the raw text
rationale: Diverse few-shot examples of the hard cases and giving Claude room to reason before answering are both established accuracy levers. Raising temperature increases variance and error on a deterministic task; leaning on parametric knowledge is the opposite of grounding; removing field descriptions strips guidance the model needs.
referenceText: Include a few diverse, relevant examples of input mapped to correct output; letting Claude think step by step before answering improves accuracy on complex tasks.

stem: You decide to add examples to your extraction prompt to lift accuracy. Which set of examples will help the most?
options: Three to five diverse examples that include the messy edge cases you actually see, each mapped to correct JSON
A single clean, ideal example so Claude is not confused by conflicting variety
Ten near-identical examples of the easiest, cleanest document type
Examples showing only the input documents, leaving Claude to produce the JSON mapping
rationale: Multishot prompting works best when a handful of examples are diverse and cover the edge cases the model must handle, each paired with the correct target output. One example underspecifies; ten near-identical easy examples teach nothing about hard cases; input-only examples fail to demonstrate the target mapping.
referenceText: Include 3-5 diverse, relevant examples that cover edge cases, each showing the desired output.

stem: On a lightweight path you call the Messages API without tools and need the assistant's first characters to be valid JSON rather than a preamble such as 'Here is the data:'. Which technique most reliably enforces that?
options: Prefill the assistant turn with an opening '{' so Claude continues the JSON object directly
Add an instruction to the user message telling Claude not to write any preamble
Set a stop sequence of 'Here is' so any preamble is cut off before the JSON
Sample the request twice and keep whichever response happens to start with a brace
rationale: Prefilling the assistant message with '{' constrains Claude to continue from that token, guaranteeing the response opens the JSON object with no preamble. A prompt instruction is not a guarantee; a 'Here is' stop sequence matches only one phrasing and is brittle; sampling twice is wasteful and still unreliable.
referenceText: Prefill Claude's response with the beginning of the desired output (e.g. '{') to skip preambles and control formatting.

stem: Extractions that require multi-step reasoning (for example, computing a total from line items) are more accurate when Claude reasons first, but you still need a clean structured payload. What is the best design?
options: Let Claude reason in an extended-thinking block or scratchpad, then emit the result through the extraction tool so the JSON stays clean
Place the chain of reasoning inside a free-text field of the same JSON object and strip it out afterward
Forbid all reasoning to minimize latency and accept the resulting accuracy loss
Request the answer twice and diff the two JSON objects to choose the more reliable one
rationale: Separating reasoning (extended thinking or a scratchpad) from the final tool call preserves accuracy while keeping the payload clean. Embedding reasoning in a JSON field pollutes the schema and risks malformed output; forbidding reasoning sacrifices the accuracy you need; double-sampling adds cost without addressing the reasoning requirement.
referenceText: Extended thinking lets Claude work through problems step by step before producing a final answer.

stem: Your app validates each tool_use payload against its JSON schema. When validation fails, what is the most effective way to let Claude fix it within the same conversation?
options: Return a tool_result marked is_error:true that describes the specific validation failures so Claude can correct the fields and retry
Silently drop the response and re-send the original request from scratch
Lower temperature and resend the same request without indicating what was wrong
Surface the error to the end user and halt the pipeline for manual correction
rationale: Returning an is_error tool_result that names the exact validation problems gives Claude the feedback it needs to self-correct on the next turn, the intended tool-use error loop. Re-sending blindly discards context and repeats the mistake; resending silently gives no signal; escalating immediately forgoes an easy automated recovery.
referenceText: If a tool call is invalid, return a tool_result with is_error set to true and a description so Claude can retry.

stem: Extracting every line item from long invoices, you sometimes receive JSON that ends abruptly and will not parse; the response's stop_reason is 'max_tokens'. What should your code do?
options: Detect stop_reason == 'max_tokens' and raise max_tokens or continue the response before treating the JSON as complete
Assume the object is essentially complete and repair the trailing brace so it parses
Lower temperature so the model produces a shorter, complete payload next time
Treat it as a transient failure and retry the identical request unchanged
rationale: A stop_reason of max_tokens means the output was truncated; the fix is to detect that reason and give the model room to finish (or continue generation) before parsing. Repairing the brace fabricates the missing items; lowering temperature does not shorten a genuinely long payload; retrying unchanged truncates again at the same limit.
referenceText: A stop_reason of max_tokens indicates the response was cut off by the token limit; increase max_tokens or continue the generation.

stem: The business requires high precision: uncertain extractions must reach a human reviewer rather than post straight to the ledger. Which design best supports this?
options: Add a confidence field to the schema, set a threshold, and route records below it to human review while auto-accepting the rest
Send every extraction to human review so no uncertain record can slip through
Auto-accept all extractions and let downstream reconciliation catch any errors
Discard any record that Claude appears unsure about to keep the ledger clean
rationale: A confidence signal plus a threshold implements an escalation policy: low-confidence records go to a human while high-confidence ones flow through, balancing precision and throughput. Reviewing everything defeats automation; auto-accepting all violates the precision requirement; discarding uncertain records loses recoverable data.
referenceText: Design agents to escalate to human review when confidence is low rather than acting on uncertain output.

stem: A nightly Agent SDK job extracts data from thousands of documents and occasionally crashes midway. Reruns must not reprocess completed documents or lose progress. What is the right approach?
options: Persist per-document status (pending/done/failed) in external durable state so a rerun resumes where it left off
Keep the list of processed documents in the conversation prompt and rely on the model to remember them
Restart from the first document on every run to guarantee nothing is missed
Track progress only in process memory for speed, accepting loss when a crash occurs
rationale: Durable external state tracking each document's status makes the job resumable and idempotent across crashes. Relying on prompt/model memory is not durable and does not scale to thousands of items; restarting from scratch wastes work and risks duplicate writes; in-memory-only state is lost on the very crashes you must survive.
referenceText: Persist progress and state externally so long-running agent jobs are resumable and idempotent rather than depending on in-context memory.

stem: One source document is far larger than the context window, yet you still need a complete extraction covering the whole document. What is the most reliable strategy?
options: Split the document into coherent sections, extract from each independently, then merge and de-duplicate the results
Truncate the document to fit the window and extract from the first portion only
Raise temperature so Claude compresses the document into fewer tokens before extracting
Send the whole document and ignore the resulting context-length error
rationale: Chunking into coherent sections, extracting per chunk, then merging and de-duplicating yields a complete result within context limits. Truncating drops data; temperature has nothing to do with fitting content in context; ignoring the error yields no extraction at all.
referenceText: When input exceeds the context window, split it into sections, process each independently, and combine the results.

stem: Under load your extraction service receives HTTP 429 (rate limited) and 529 (overloaded) responses from the API. What is the correct client behavior?
options: Retry with exponential backoff and jitter, honoring any Retry-After hint, up to a bounded number of attempts
Retry immediately in a tight loop until the request eventually succeeds
Treat 429 and 529 as permanent failures and drop the affected document
Switch to a larger model on each 429 to work around the limit
rationale: 429 and 529 are transient; the correct response is bounded exponential backoff with jitter, respecting Retry-After. Tight-loop retries worsen congestion; treating them as permanent discards recoverable work; switching models neither fixes rate limiting nor addresses overload and can raise cost.
referenceText: 429 rate_limit and 529 overloaded errors are transient; retry with exponential backoff.

stem: You want CI to fail automatically whenever a prompt change causes Claude to emit output that violates the extraction schema. Which setup fits best?
options: Run Claude Code headlessly (claude -p) over fixture documents in CI and fail the job if any output fails schema validation
Manually spot-check a handful of outputs before each release
Skip schema validation in CI because it slows the build and validate later
Validate outputs only in production so the CI pipeline stays fast
rationale: Headless Claude Code (claude -p) runs non-interactively in CI, so you can extract against fixtures and fail the build on schema violations, catching regressions before release. Manual spot checks are not reproducible gates; skipping validation in CI or validating only in production lets regressions ship.
referenceText: Use headless mode (claude -p) to run Claude Code non-interactively in CI and scripts.

stem: You expose the extractor as an MCP server tool so several internal clients can reuse it. Which statement about the tool's interface is correct?
options: Each MCP tool declares a JSON Schema inputSchema that describes and constrains its arguments for any connecting client
MCP tools cannot advertise schemas, so each client must guess the expected arguments
MCP tool arguments are always free-form strings with no declared structure
An MCP tool can be invoked only by the client that originally registered it
rationale: MCP tools advertise a name, description, and JSON Schema inputSchema, so any connecting client/host knows and can validate the expected arguments, which is what makes the extractor reusable. The second and third options contradict the tool definition; the fourth misstates MCP, where a server exposes tools for any authorized client to call.
referenceText: Each tool is defined with a name, description, and an inputSchema (JSON Schema) describing its parameters.

stem: Your schema's status enum is ["Open","Closed"], but the database column expects lowercase, and Claude occasionally returns 'open'. Structured outputs are enabled. What is the robust fix?
options: Case-fold the enum value before insert, because exact capitalization of enum values is not guaranteed
Trust that casing always matches the schema and insert the returned value verbatim
Remove the enum constraint so any string is accepted and normalized later
Raise max_tokens so Claude has room to reproduce the exact enum casing
rationale: Structured outputs constrain values to the enum set but do not guarantee exact capitalization, so normalizing before insert is the robust integration step. Trusting exact casing breaks the insert intermittently; removing the enum sacrifices validation; max_tokens is unrelated to casing.
referenceText: Structured outputs don't guarantee the capitalization of string enum and const values; compare enum values case-insensitively.

stem: Which two design choices help your extractor degrade gracefully on unexpected or malformed documents rather than fail hard? (Select 2.)
options: Use nullable fields and instruct Claude to return null for information the document does not contain
Return validation errors to Claude via an is_error tool_result so it can self-correct
Configure the schema to force a best-guess value for every field even when the data is absent
Abort the entire batch the first time a single document fails to parse
Disable schema validation so that no output is ever rejected
rationale: Nullable fields with a null-when-absent instruction, plus an is_error feedback loop that lets Claude fix invalid output, together let the system handle edge cases without crashing. Forcing best-guess values manufactures wrong data; aborting the whole batch on one failure is the opposite of graceful degradation; disabling validation removes the safety net entirely.
referenceText: Return a tool_result with is_error true so Claude can correct invalid output; use nullable fields for information that may be absent.

stem: Claude's final turn should return structured data to the caller as its own response (it is not invoking one of your executable functions). Which structured-output mechanism fits best?
options: Use the JSON output format (output_config.format) to constrain Claude's response to your schema
Define a dummy tool and force it even though no function will actually execute
Extract the fields from a free-text response with a regular expression
Prefill the complete JSON yourself and discard whatever Claude generates
rationale: When you want Claude's own response, not a function call, to match a schema, the JSON output format (output_config.format) is the purpose-built feature; strict tool use is for validating calls to functions you execute. A dummy forced tool is a workaround that muddles intent; regex parsing is brittle; prefilling everything discards the extraction you requested.
referenceText: JSON outputs control Claude's response format; strict tool use validates parameters of tool calls. They solve different problems.

stem: Several engineers run the extraction task from Claude Code and get inconsistent field naming because each writes their own prompt. What is the best way to standardize it?
options: Codify the schema and extraction instructions in a project slash command backed by CLAUDE.md so every run uses the same definition
Ask each engineer to memorize the schema and paste it manually at the start of each run
Raise temperature so the divergent prompts converge on a common output shape
Rely on the model to infer a consistent schema across the different users' prompts
rationale: A shared project slash command backed by CLAUDE.md gives everyone one authoritative, version-controlled prompt and schema, eliminating per-engineer drift. Manual pasting reintroduces inconsistency; temperature cannot reconcile divergent prompts; hoping the model infers consistency is exactly the failure being observed.
referenceText: Define reusable prompts as project slash commands and shared instructions in CLAUDE.md so the team runs them consistently.

stem: Your extraction service must return only a JSON object conforming to a fixed schema (invoice number, vendor, line items, total) so a downstream ERP can ingest it without a parsing layer. Which approach most reliably guarantees the model emits data matching that structure?
options: Define a tool whose input_schema is the target JSON schema, force it with tool_choice, and read the structured tool_use input
Add a system-prompt line instructing the model to respond with valid JSON only and never include prose, then json.loads the text block
Set temperature to 0 so the model deterministically produces the same JSON shape on every call
Append a stop_sequence of '}' so generation halts as soon as the JSON object closes
rationale: Forcing a specific tool via tool_choice makes the model return its output as a structured tool_use block whose input is validated against the tool's input_schema, the canonical way to guarantee schema-shaped output. A prose instruction is not enforced and frequently leaks explanatory text or markdown fences; temperature 0 reduces variability but does not constrain structure; a stop_sequence of '}' truncates any object containing nested braces.
referenceText: By forcing Claude to use a tool, you can guarantee that its response conforms to the tool's input_schema.

stem: You are extracting without tool use and want the very first token of the response to begin the JSON object so no leading prose appears. Which technique most directly forces this?
options: Prefill the assistant turn with an opening '{' so the model continues the JSON from that point
Increase max_tokens so the model has room to finish the object before any commentary
Set top_p to 0.1 to suppress the model's tendency to add explanatory text
Emphasize in the user message that the output must start with a brace and contain no preamble
rationale: Prefilling the assistant message with '{' constrains the model to continue from that character, eliminating preamble and forcing JSON to begin immediately. Raising max_tokens only affects length; lowering top_p changes sampling diversity but does not remove leading text; a prompt instruction nudges but does not guarantee the first token.
referenceText: You can pre-fill part of Claude's response in the assistant turn to guide it to skip preambles and output in a specific format.

stem: A downstream JSON Schema validator rejects roughly 3% of extractions (wrong types, missing required keys). You want a resilient pipeline that recovers automatically without a human. What is the most effective reliability pattern?
options: Discard any record that fails validation and log it so the batch can still finish on schedule
Loosen the schema to make every field optional so validation always passes
Return the specific validator error message to the model, ask it to correct the extraction, and retry up to a bounded number of times
Resubmit the identical request at temperature 0 until it validates
rationale: Feeding the concrete validation error back to the model gives it the exact defect to fix, and a bounded retry loop prevents runaway cost while recovering most transient failures. Discarding records loses data the task requires. Loosening the schema defeats validation and lets bad data reach downstream systems. Resubmitting an identical prompt at temperature 0 deterministically reproduces the same failure.
referenceText: When a tool result or downstream check fails, return the error to Claude so it can adjust and retry rather than failing the whole run.

stem: Some source documents omit the customer's tax ID. Your schema currently marks tax_id as a required string, and the model sometimes invents a plausible-looking ID when it is absent. What is the best schema and prompting fix?
options: Keep tax_id required and add a few-shot example containing a representative tax ID so the model matches the pattern
Make tax_id nullable (type ["string", "null"]) and instruct the model to return null when the value is absent from the document
Remove tax_id from the schema entirely so the model cannot populate it incorrectly
Raise max_tokens so the model has room to reason toward the correct ID
rationale: A nullable field plus an explicit instruction to emit null for absent data gives the model a correct, non-hallucinated way to represent missing information while keeping the field in the contract. A few-shot example of a populated ID does nothing when the datum is missing. Removing the field loses a value present in most documents. More tokens do not help when the datum simply is not in the source.
referenceText: Give Claude a way to say a value is absent, such as a null or 'not found' option, to reduce hallucination.

stem: Accuracy is poor on documents with unusual layouts and ambiguous phrasing. Without changing the model or schema, which two prompt-engineering techniques most directly improve extraction quality on these edge cases? (Select 2.)
options: Include few-shot examples that specifically cover the tricky layouts and their correct structured output
Increase temperature so the model explores more interpretations of ambiguous passages
Remove field descriptions from the schema so the model is not biased toward a particular reading
Let the model reason step by step about the document before it emits the final structured output
rationale: Few-shot examples that mirror the hard cases teach the desired mapping directly, and allowing step-by-step reasoning before the structured output improves correctness on ambiguous inputs. Raising temperature increases variance and makes extraction less reliable. Stripping field descriptions removes guidance the model uses to place values correctly.
referenceText: Providing examples (multishot prompting) and letting Claude think step by step both improve accuracy on complex tasks.

stem: A category field is an enum of ['hardware','software','services']. Occasionally a document describes something that fits none of these, and the model force-fits the closest value, corrupting analytics. What is the cleanest fix?
options: Convert category to a free-text string so the model records whatever it observes
Instruct the model to default to the most common category whenever it is unsure
Remove the enum constraint and rely on the downstream system to normalize the value
Add an explicit 'other' (or 'unknown') member to the enum and instruct the model to use it when no listed category applies
rationale: An explicit 'other'/'unknown' enum member gives the model a valid escape hatch, preserving a closed vocabulary while preventing forced miscategorization. Free text destroys the controlled vocabulary analytics depend on. Defaulting to the most common category deliberately injects wrong data. Dropping the enum pushes the ambiguity downstream instead of resolving it at extraction.
referenceText: Enums with a catch-all value let the model represent out-of-vocabulary cases without inventing data.

stem: You must extract structured records from 50,000 archived contracts overnight. Latency per document does not matter, but per-document cost does, and the job can complete any time before morning. Which API approach best fits?
options: Open 50,000 concurrent streaming requests so the job finishes as quickly as possible
Submit the documents through the Message Batches API for asynchronous, lower-cost processing
Send the documents one at a time synchronously to keep the code simple
Combine all 50,000 documents into a single request to amortize per-call overhead
rationale: The Message Batches API is designed for large, latency-tolerant workloads and offers reduced cost, matching an overnight bulk job exactly. Massive concurrent streaming maximizes speed and rate-limit pressure, not cost efficiency. Sequential synchronous calls are simple but slow and no cheaper. Packing everything into one request would exceed the context window and lose per-document isolation.
referenceText: The Message Batches API processes large volumes of requests asynchronously at reduced cost, ideal for non-time-sensitive workloads.

stem: Every extraction request includes the same lengthy system prompt, the full JSON schema, and a large block of few-shot examples; only the document text changes. You want to cut cost and latency on this repeated prefix. What should you use?
options: Move the schema and examples into a fine-tuned model so they no longer need to be sent
Truncate the few-shot examples to a single example to shrink each request
Enable prompt caching on the static prefix (instructions, schema, and examples) so it is reused across requests
Send the static content once in an initial request and rely on the model to retain it for later requests
rationale: Prompt caching stores the large, unchanging prefix so subsequent requests reuse it at lower cost and latency, which is exactly this pattern. Fine-tuning is far heavier than needed. Cutting examples degrades accuracy to save cost. The API is stateless between requests, so the model cannot retain prior content.
referenceText: Prompt caching lets you reuse large, static portions of your prompt across requests, reducing cost and latency.

stem: A subset of your documents are 400-page regulatory filings that exceed the model's context window even after trimming. You still need complete structured extraction from them. What is the soundest strategy?
options: Split each filing into coherent sections, extract structured data per section, then merge the partial results into one validated record
Truncate each filing to the first pages that fit and extract only from those
Summarize the entire filing first, then extract fields from the short summary
Resubmit the full document repeatedly until a request happens to fit the window
rationale: Chunking into meaningful sections, extracting per chunk, and merging preserves full coverage while respecting the context limit. Truncation drops data that may contain required fields. Summarizing first is lossy and can discard the exact values you need to extract verbatim. Retrying an over-length request never makes it fit; it simply fails repeatedly.
referenceText: Requests that exceed the context window must be split; process long documents in chunks and combine the results.

stem: Your invoices arrive as scanned image PDFs with no embedded text layer. Your current pipeline runs a lossy OCR step to plain text before extraction, and numbers in cramped tables are frequently misread. How can you improve fidelity with the model itself?
options: Increase the OCR engine's DPI and continue feeding the resulting text to the model
Instruct the model to infer the most likely digits when the OCR output looks garbled
Convert the PDF to a bulleted text summary before sending it to the model
Pass the PDF directly to the model using document content blocks so it reads the page layout and text natively
rationale: The model's PDF support lets it read the document, including visual layout and tables, directly, bypassing the lossy OCR bottleneck corrupting the numbers. Tuning OCR DPI still routes everything through the same lossy pre-step. Instructing the model to infer digits invites fabrication. Summarizing to bullets discards the precise tabular values extraction needs.
referenceText: Claude can process PDF documents directly, understanding both text and visual layout without a separate OCR step.

stem: Auditors require that every extracted contract value can be traced to the exact sentence in the source document it came from. Which model capability provides this grounding directly?
options: Instruct the model to append the page number it believes each value came from as an extra field
Enable Citations so the model attaches references to the source passages that support each extracted value
Log the full model response and have reviewers search the document for each value afterward
Lower the temperature so the model copies source text more literally
rationale: The Citations feature returns verifiable references to the source spans that support the output, giving auditors traceability by design. Self-reported page numbers are unverified and can be hallucinated. Manual post-hoc search does not scale and is error-prone. Low temperature affects wording, not traceability.
referenceText: Citations lets Claude provide detailed references to the exact sentences and passages it uses to generate responses.

stem: Your extraction prompt concatenates lengthy instructions, the JSON schema, and the raw document into one string, and the model sometimes treats instructions embedded in the document as commands. What is the recommended way to delimit the source document?
options: Prefix the document with a capitalized DOCUMENT label so the model recognizes the boundary
Base64-encode the document so its text cannot be interpreted as instructions
Wrap the document in clearly named XML tags (for example <document>...</document>) to separate content from instructions
Place the document before all instructions so the model reads the content first
rationale: XML tags are the documented technique for structurally separating input content from instructions, reducing instruction bleed-through. A capitalized label is far weaker and easily ignored. Base64 encoding makes the document unreadable to the model, defeating extraction. Reordering does not create a clear boundary between data and instructions.
referenceText: Use XML tags to clearly separate different parts of your prompt, such as instructions, examples, and input data.

stem: Business rules say extractions the model is unsure about must go to a human reviewer, while confident ones flow straight through. How should you design the extraction contract to support this routing?
options: Route a fixed random 10% of all records to human review regardless of the model's certainty
Send every record to a human first and use the model only to double-check the reviewer's work
Use request latency as a proxy, treating slower responses as lower-confidence extractions
Add a numeric confidence field to the schema, have the model populate it, and route records below a threshold to human review
rationale: A model-populated confidence field plus a threshold gives targeted, per-record escalation aligned with the business rule. Random sampling escalates the wrong records and misses genuinely uncertain ones. Human-first review discards the automation benefit. Latency does not correlate with correctness and is a meaningless confidence signal.
referenceText: Design structured outputs to include signals like confidence so uncertain results can be escalated for human review.

stem: You are building a production extraction service that must call tools, validate results, and retry on failure within a managed agent loop, and you want to build it in TypeScript or Python without hand-rolling the orchestration. Which foundation is the best fit?
options: Build on the Claude Agent SDK, which provides the agent loop, tool orchestration, and error-handling primitives
Write a bespoke while-loop around raw HTTP calls and manage every retry and tool dispatch yourself
Drive extractions through interactive Claude Code and copy each result out by hand
Issue a single-shot completion call with no loop and accept whatever it returns
rationale: The Agent SDK provides the harness (agent loop, tool-use orchestration, error handling) so you can build a reliable extraction service without reimplementing that machinery. A hand-rolled loop reinvents what the SDK already provides and is error-prone. Interactive Claude Code is not a production service. A single-shot call has no retry or tool orchestration for a robust pipeline.
referenceText: The Claude Agent SDK provides the building blocks (the agent loop, tool use, and error handling) for building production agents.

stem: Several internal applications (a chat app, a batch job, an IDE assistant) need to run the same document-extraction capability, and you want it reusable across clients without duplicating logic. Which Model Context Protocol construct best exposes it?
options: Paste the extraction prompt into each application's codebase separately
Expose the documents as MCP resources and let each client author its own extraction prompt
Implement the extraction capability as an MCP tool on a server so any MCP-compatible client can invoke it
Publish the schema to a shared repository and let each team wire up its own extraction logic
rationale: MCP tools are the standard way to expose an executable capability that any MCP client can discover and call, giving reuse across apps. Copy-pasting the prompt duplicates and diverges logic. Exposing only the documents as resources still forces each client to reimplement extraction. A shared schema provides no shared runtime capability.
referenceText: Tools in MCP let servers expose executable functionality that clients can discover and invoke.

stem: You want your extraction workflow to run non-interactively in a CI job: read documents from a directory, produce JSON, and fail the build if validation fails. Which Claude Code capability enables this?
options: Keep a developer signed into an interactive Claude Code session for the duration of each CI run
Run Claude Code in headless mode with the -p (print) flag so it executes non-interactively and emits output the CI job can consume
Parse the interactive TUI output captured in the CI logs
Trigger the extraction from CI but have a human run it and paste the results back
rationale: Headless mode (claude -p) is designed for scripted, non-interactive automation such as CI pipelines, producing output a build can act on. Requiring an interactive human session defeats automation. Scraping TUI output is brittle and unsupported. Having a human run it removes the automation entirely.
referenceText: Use headless mode (the -p flag) to run Claude Code non-interactively in scripts, CI, and other automation.

stem: Your extraction repo has a canonical schema at schemas/invoice.json, a required validation command, and a rule that new fields must be nullable. You want Claude Code to follow these conventions automatically on every task in the repo. Where should you record them?
options: In a comment at the top of the main source file so Claude encounters it while editing
In the repository's pull request template so reviewers can point Claude to them
In each engineer's personal notes kept outside the repository
In a CLAUDE.md file at the repo root so Claude Code loads the conventions as project memory each session
rationale: CLAUDE.md is the documented project-memory file that Claude Code loads automatically, making it the right home for schema location and extraction conventions. A comment in one source file is not reliably discovered. A PR template only helps humans after work is done. Notes outside the repo are never seen by Claude Code.
referenceText: CLAUDE.md files are automatically loaded into context and are the place to record project conventions and commands.

stem: You force a single extraction tool, but on some documents the model returns two parallel tool_use blocks (partial records), breaking your one-record-per-document assumption. Which request setting most directly prevents multiple simultaneous tool calls?
options: Set disable_parallel_tool_use to true in tool_choice so the model emits at most one tool call per turn
Lower max_tokens so there is only room for a single tool_use block
Add a stop_sequence after the first tool call to cut off any second block
Raise temperature so the model commits to a single interpretation
rationale: disable_parallel_tool_use in tool_choice constrains the model to a single tool call per turn, directly enforcing one record per document. Shrinking max_tokens risks truncating even the first record. A stop_sequence cannot reliably target a second tool_use block. Higher temperature increases variability rather than constraining call count.
referenceText: Set disable_parallel_tool_use to true to prevent Claude from calling multiple tools in a single response.

stem: The model keeps inventing values for fields that are simply not present in some documents. Which two changes most directly reduce these hallucinated fields? (Select 2.)
options: Raise max_tokens so the model has more room to complete every field
Explicitly instruct the model to return null (or 'not found') for any field whose value is absent from the document
Provide clear, specific descriptions for each schema field so the model knows exactly what to look for
Add few-shot examples that consistently show fully populated documents
rationale: Giving the model an explicit way to signal absence (null / 'not found') removes the pressure to fabricate, and precise field descriptions reduce misattribution. More max_tokens does nothing about hallucination and can encourage the model to keep filling. Examples that are always fully populated teach the model that every field should be present, increasing fabrication on sparse documents.
referenceText: To reduce hallucinations, give Claude an out to say a value is not found, and describe exactly what each field should contain.

stem: Your extraction pipeline has multiple stages (classify document type, extract fields, cross-check totals). Between stages you currently pass the model's free-form prose, and later stages misparse it. What is the most reliable way to carry state between stages?
options: Concatenate each stage's full natural-language response and pass it forward as one growing string
Re-run the entire extraction from scratch at each stage to avoid passing state
Persist each stage's output as a validated structured JSON state object that the next stage consumes programmatically
Store only the final stage's output and reconstruct earlier state as needed
rationale: Passing validated structured JSON state between stages makes inter-stage handoffs machine-parseable and reliable, avoiding the ambiguity of prose. Accumulating free-form text is exactly what is breaking parsing. Re-running everything each stage wastes cost and discards accumulated results. Storing only the final output loses the intermediate state later stages need.
referenceText: Maintain state between steps as structured data rather than free-form text so each step can reliably consume the previous one's output.
